EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over the Central Plains moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley during the evening hours of Friday, March 14, 2025. Increasing moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front lifted dewpoints into the low to mid 60s. This coupled with increasing height falls and a mid-level 80 knot jet rotating around the main upper low supported severe thunderstorm development. Large looping hodographs, MLCAPE values over 2000 J/kg, 60 knots of bulk shear, and 0-1 km SRH values between 250 and 350 m2/s2 supported an all hazards threat. Storm mode during the overnight hours was supercellular with large hail, damaging winds and several tornadoes, a few of which were strong (EF2+), mainly across northeast Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel and northeast Mississippi. ||The main upper low lifted into the western Great Lakes Region by early Saturday morning, March 15, 2025. The cold front stalled just to the west of the Mississippi River. Simultaneously, a neutrally-tilted longwave trough moved across the Southern Plains and pushed a 100 knot mid-level jet into the region. This placed West Tennessee and  north Mississippi beneath the left exit region of the jet with widespread lift and diffluence available. As a result, showers and thunderstorms rapidly redeveloped across north Mississippi and parts of West Tennessee Saturday morning. Storms quickly formed into a line that slowly moved east. The line of storms produced damaging winds, large hail and a weak tornado Saturday morning. The airmass ahead of the line became even more favorable for severe storms by Saturday afternoon with MLCAPE values increasing to 1500 J/kg and 0-1km SRH values reaching 300 m2/s2 as a surface low moving along the cold front and backed surface winds across northeast Mississippi.  Discrete supercells formed ahead of the line during the afternoon producing a couple of weak tornadoes and damaging winds over parts of northeast Missisisppi. Flash flooding was also common due to the slow moving nature of the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado developed near Callahan Road lifting a few miles to the east along Wellington Road just south of Cub Creek Tree Farm Lake. Only minor tree damage was noted along the short path. Peak winds were estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stretched from Lower Michigan back through the Mid-South during the early morning hours of March 16, 2025. A combination of steep mid-level lapse rates and some elevated instability produced pea to nickel-size hail across a few West Tennessee locations overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed as a warm front lifted north across West Tennessee. Enough instability and low-level shear were present to encourage the development of a few mini super cells. One mini supercell produced wind damage north of Dyersburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was ripped off an outbuilding, and a tree was knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system tracked across the eastern United States, steering a very strong low-level jet over Middle Tennessee on March 4, 2025. These very strong upper-level winds were able to mix down to the surface which resulted in damaging winds across the area. The strongest winds during this event were measured across the Cumberland Plateau where wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph occurred. While wind gusts weren't as strong west of the Cumberland Plateau, several hours of strong winds still caused damage. Across Middle Tennessee, numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines along with power outages were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong non-thunderstorm winds across White County downed numerous trees across the area. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong non-thunderstorm winds across Wilson County caused several downed trees. Near Green Hill, a tree fell along Lebanon Road near South Greenhill Road, causing the eastbound lane to be blocked. Another large tree was also blown down onto Lebanon Road in Green Hill. Observed wind gusts around the time were between 40 to 55 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low moving into the Midwest helped intensify a very strong low level jet that brought high end mountain wave winds to the East Tennessee mountains and foothills, with strong non-thunderstorm wind gusts that were sub-warning criteria across much of the Tennessee Valley. An event of this magnitude is typically rare per each winter season. After the passage of the cold front, light snow showers brought light snow and limited impacts to higher elevations of the East Tennessee Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain NPS fire tower recorded a peak non-thunderstorm wind gust of 96 mph during several hours of high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place across the Mid-South ahead of an approaching cold front. Dewpoints ranged from 75 to 80 degrees across the area. As the cold front sagged south, an upper-level disturbance pushed into the region, causing an increase in shear. Convection developed along an old outflow boundary during the late afternoon and evening hours on June 18, 2025. Additional development occurred as the front and additional outflow boundaries pushed south into the area. Storm mode was generally clusters and segments at first. Robust instability and high DCAPE values supported large hail and damaging winds initially. After midnight and during the early morning hours of June 19, 2025, the front nearly stalled and became parallel to the upper-level flow. As a result, convection became more east-west oriented with very heavy rainfall rates. Training storms caused significant flash flooding over parts of West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding throughout the Covington area and across much of Tipton County. Over 7 inches of rain fell in a short period of time, causing flooding on Highway 59 and Highway 51, along with South Main Street in Covington. There were multiple water rescues. Thirty-two homes and businesses were flooded. Many roads and bridges were damaged throughout the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peavine Road between Reeves Road and Bass Road was washed away by flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place across the Mid-South ahead of an approaching cold front. Dewpoints ranged from 75 to 80 degrees across the area. As the cold front sagged south, an upper-level disturbance pushed into the region, causing an increase in shear. Convection developed along an old outflow boundary during the late afternoon and evening hours on June 18, 2025. Additional development occurred as the front and additional outflow boundaries pushed south into the area. Storm mode was generally clusters and segments at first. Robust instability and high DCAPE values supported large hail and damaging winds initially. After midnight and during the early morning hours of June 19, 2025, the front nearly stalled and became parallel to the upper-level flow. As a result, convection became more east-west oriented with very heavy rainfall rates. Training storms caused significant flash flooding over parts of West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: I-240 at Jefferson Rd was completely flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of thunderstorms and severe weather to Middle Tennessee on March 15, 2025. The main risk for severe weather was associated with a line of thunderstorms that tracks east across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours. This line produced one EF-1 tornado near Christiana. As this line moved towards the Cumberland Plateau, it strengthened and caused wind damage in numerous locations. Some of the worst wind damage was found in Palmer where an estimated swath of 95 mph winds uprooted trees and caused structural damage. Numerous downed trees and structural damage was also found in White, Fentress, and Overton counties. These multiple rounds of rain also caused flooding issues with flash flooding and reports of road closures reported across Middle Tennessee. This heavy rain also caused several rivers to rise into flood stage for days after this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shoal Creek near Iron City crested to 19.9 feet which was moderate flood stage. At this stage, water flooded portions of Railroad Road east of Highway 242 and approached Wayland Spring Road and Highway 242 north of Iron City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of March 30, 2025, and into the early morning of March 31, 2025. With ample moisture, instability, and wind shear in place, the stage was set for severe weather. Thunderstorm wind damage was common with several reports of downed trees and other structure damage received. Large hail up to 2 inches in diameter was also reported, especially across Williamson County. This event was also responsible for four tornadoes with the strongest being an EF-2 tornado near Hampshire in Maury County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down in western Maury County, just northeast of Hampshire near U.S. Highway 412. It was here that timber damage occurred as well as damage to at least one barn on Old State Road and Roy Thompson Road. The tornado crossed into an inaccessible wooded area thereafter then emerged onto Rippey Lake Road, causing additional damage to trees. The tornado then caused significant damage to a home, removing almost the entire roof section and collapsing most walls. The tornado weakened as it moved southeast, causing additional timber damage on South Bridges Road before dissipating on Ashwood Road. Damages are a rough estimate.

Water approached structures along South Cannon Boulevard between the Highway 64 and Lewis Avenue junction and the bridge at Fisherman's Park, parking areas and other property of St. William Catholic Church, and the Tyson plant on West Jackson Street.

This line produced one EF-1 tornado near Christiana. As this line moved towards the Cumberland Plateau, it strengthened and caused wind damage in numerous locations. Some of the worst wind damage was found in Palmer where an estimated swath of 95 mph winds uprooted trees and caused structural damage. Numerous downed trees and structural damage was also found in White, Fentress, and Overton counties. These multiple rounds of rain also caused flooding issues with flash flooding and reports of road closures reported across Middle Tennessee. This heavy rain also caused several rivers to rise into flood stage for days after this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mill Creek near Nolensville crested to 14.18 feet which was minor flood stage. At this stage, water flooded low-lying areas along the creek, including portions of Culbertson Road east of Nolensville Pike as well as walking trails near the Honey Brook subdivision. Water also approached Bluff Road west of Nolensville Pike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Duck River at Centerville crested to 24.62 feet which was minor flood stage. At this stage, flood waters covered the flood plain along the river. Leatherwood Road between Shady Grove and Primm Springs near Tottys Bend were also impacted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado first touched down in far northwest Bedford County near Carter Crossroads on Kingdom Road. At this location, a half dozen outbuildings, storage buildings, and barns were damaged or destroyed. The tornado continued east-northeast where some minor damage occurred to homes along Longview Road. Garage doors were blown in, fences were knocked down, outbuildings were destroyed, and minor roof damage was observed. The tornado then continued into far southwest Rutherford County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team determined that the damage near Palmer was caused by straight-line winds with maximum wind speeds of 95 mph. The width of the damage path was determined to be over a mile wide. These very strong downsloping microburst winds blew straight into Palmer, causing many trees to be uprooted and snapped along with some structural damage in the area. All trees fell in generally the same direction with no narrow tornado path found. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of March 30, 2025, and into the early morning of March 31, 2025. With ample moisture, instability, and wind shear in place, the stage was set for severe weather. Thunderstorm wind damage was common with several reports of downed trees and other structure damage received. Large hail up to 2 inches in diameter was also reported, especially across Williamson County. This event was also responsible for four tornadoes with the strongest being an EF-2 tornado near Hampshire in Maury County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed trees and powerlines along Highway 64 near the Hardin County line. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system tracked across the eastern United States, steering a very strong low-level jet over Middle Tennessee on March 4, 2025. These very strong upper-level winds were able to mix down to the surface which resulted in damaging winds across the area. The strongest winds during this event were measured across the Cumberland Plateau where wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph occurred. While wind gusts weren't as strong west of the Cumberland Plateau, several hours of strong winds still caused damage. Across Middle Tennessee, numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines along with power outages were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong non-thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees onto roadways across several areas in Lawrence County, including Summertown, Ethridge, and St. Joseph. Observed wind gusts around that time were between 40 to 55 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

Roof damage was also reported to an auto repair shop on Washington Avenue in Cookeville. A tree also fell on an RV along Crossville Highway. No injuries were reported. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active weather pattern continued across Middle Tennessee during March 2025 which continued to improve drought conditions across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air filtered into southern middle Tennessee behind a cold front during the evening into the overnight hours on Tuesday, February 18th. As precipitation developed along and just north of the front during the evening hours, it fell as light snowfall. This precipitation continued until a few hours after daybreak on Wednesday (February the 19th) as light or moderate snowfall. One to two inches of snowfall accumulation was common across northern Alabama after this activity ended early Wednesday morning. Temperatures continued to be below freezing much of the day, keeping roads icy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure cyclone passing to the south with upper level forcing induced a broad and significant snowstorm across the region, including East Tennessee, which experienced heavy snowfall. A frontogenesis band across southern Tennessee and northern Georgia helped power higher snowfall rates early into the event across southeastern Tennessee, resulting in locally heavier snowfall before warm air advection brought a changeover in precipitation type to icing and eventually to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the early afternoon, generally small flakes. Then between 5 and 630 was a period of light sleet/freezing rain mix, snow then came in and light snow continued until  gradually lessening and stopping before 11 PM. Measured total was 2.3 inches of snow with a SWE of 0.28.

A warm nose did creep into parts of north Mississippi resulting in a period of heavy sleet. Snow and sleet totals generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches across the Mid-South with locally higher amounts. The greatest snow totals were found in the Memphis Metropolitan area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air and moisture advection into Middle Tennessee on February 6, 2025, helped to set the stage for thunderstorms to develop across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. These thunderstorms tended to track eastward along a boundary that had set up near the Tennessee and Kentucky border. One particular thunderstorm intensified rapidly as it tracked eastward towards the Cumberland Plateau. The thunderstorm interacting with the boundary combined with the terrain of the Cumberland Plateau likely allowed it to begin rotating and become tornadic. This one thunderstorm cycled several times and produced four tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-2 tornado touched down just east of Highway 56 north of Center Grove in Jackson County where dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted as well as some minor roof damage to a single family|home. The tornado continued east-northeast and caused significant roof damage to a manufactured home along JC Johnson Lane. From here, the tornado crossed Seven Knobs Road where it intensified, causing significant timber damage west of Blackburn Fork Road. Even more trees were snapped and uprooted as the tornado continued east along Lynn Hill Road. A barn was destroyed along Zion Road before dissipating just east of that area. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air and moisture advection into Middle Tennessee on February 6, 2025, helped to set the stage for thunderstorms to develop across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. One particular thunderstorm intensified rapidly as it tracked eastward towards the Cumberland Plateau. The thunderstorm interacting with the boundary combined with the terrain of the Cumberland Plateau likely allowed it to begin rotating and become tornadic. This one thunderstorm cycled several times and produced four tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-1 tornado touched down near Cyphers Drive in northern Putnam County where tree damage was found. As the tornado continued east, there was additional tree damage found along Cindy Drive along with roof damage and significant garage damage to homes on Malone Lane. The tornado then crossed into Overton County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast from the northwest Gulf of America on the 9th and through southeast Louisiana into south Alabama by the 10th. Moisture surging north as the low developed produced widespread wintry precipitation across the mid South and Tennessee Valley. Snow enveloped north Alabama and southern middle Tennessee just after midnight into the morning hours of the 10th. Some of the snow was heavy at times.  A wet snow accumulation of 2 to 6 inches fell across southern middle Tennessee, before mixing with sleet and then changing to rain or freezing rain. Temperatures warmed into the lower 30s, in many cases at or just above 32 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5.4 inches was reported near Winchester and 4.0 inches in Sewanee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado that began in Putnam County. This EF-1 tornado then uprooted and snapped trees along Duncans Chapel Road as well as caused minor roof damage and downed trees on Ben Mason Road. Further east along Tommy Dodson Highway, several trees were uprooted and two barns had roof damage, and a barn was destroyed. On Highway 111, there was damage to a gas station and retail|building and more trees damaged near Rickman. Minor roof damage to a barn and some branches found along Okalona Road east of Rickman before the tornado dissipated. This tornado was responsible for one injury in Overton County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-1 tornado touched down near Coy Phillips Road, west of Hanging Limb in Overton County. The tornado continued along E Fork Lane where it caused significant roofing damage to a residence and uprooted trees. Along Steel Tram Ridge Lane, the tornado completely ripped a porch off the front of a house and caused a camper to roll on its side. The tornado presumably lifted near the end of Indian Creek Trail east of Hanging Limb. This tornado was also responsible for one injury in Overton County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting led to a broad stratiform area of precipitation that began as a wintry mix before transitioning to rain. As the cold front approached, strengthening pressure gradient across the mountains led to notable high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light snowfall transitioned to light freezing rain across Hawkins County. In Church Hill, at least 0.6 inches of snow was reported, along with a glaze of ice from sleet and freezing rain. In Okolona, a trained spotter reported an event total of 1.5 inches of snow along with a glaze of ice on numerous outdoor surfaces, resulting in snow and ice covered roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting led to a broad stratiform area of precipitation that began as a wintry mix before transitioning to rain. As the cold front approached, strengthening pressure gradient across the mountains led to notable high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light snow that began in the morning transitioned to wintry mix by the early afternoon before entering a lull until the evening hours. Highest snowfall report was 1.2 inches in Bristol with a light glaze of ice. 0.9 inches of snow was reported in Bluff City, 0.5 inches of snow in Kingsport, and an inch of snow in Blountville. Wintry mix during the evening resulted in an additional light glaze of ice measured in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting led to a broad stratiform area of precipitation that began as a wintry mix before transitioning to rain. As the cold front approached, strengthening pressure gradient across the mountains led to notable high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A several hour period of high non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred at the Cove Mountain Fire Tower in the Great Smoky Mountains National Park. The 8 pm observation first hit high wind criteria, recording a 61 mph wind gust. Peak gust was recorded on the 1 AM observation with a magnitude of 99 mph. Another 90 mph wind gust occurred on a later observation. The winds were below high wind criteria at the 4 AM observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting led to a broad stratiform area of precipitation that began as a wintry mix before transitioning to rain. As the cold front approached, strengthening pressure gradient across the mountains led to notable high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sterchi UMC Lodge at 4420 feet on Max Patch Mountain recorded a 65 mph wind gust on a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Gulf Low developed along the southeast Texas coast and tracked northeastward into southern and central Mississippi and Alabama on January 9-10, 2025. The Mid-South remained on the cold side of the low resulting in heavy snowfall across most of the Mid-South. A warm nose did creep into parts of north Mississippi resulting in a period of heavy sleet. Snow and sleet totals generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches across the Mid-South with locally higher amounts. The greatest snow totals were found in the Memphis Metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snowfall observed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting led to a broad stratiform area of precipitation that began as a wintry mix before transitioning to rain. As the cold front approached, strengthening pressure gradient across the mountains led to notable high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light wintry mix resulted in 0.6 inches of snow being measured in Tazewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting led to a broad stratiform area of precipitation that began as a wintry mix before transitioning to rain. As the cold front approached, strengthening pressure gradient across the mountains led to notable high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light snow that transitioned over to freezing rain by late morning resulted in at least a half inch of snow in the county, with roads covered by the snow. After changeover to freezing rain, a coating of ice was recorded across many outdoor surfaces, resulting in icy and slick surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm impacted all of Middle Tennessee on January 10, 2025. With cold air already in place, a strong low pressure system and plenty of moisture brought heavy snow to the area. The heaviest snow was reported generally west of Interstate 24 where 4 to 6 inches of snow fell. Snow rates dropped closer to the Cumberland Plateau which generally reported 1 to 3 inches of snow. Regardless, major travel impacts were reported areawide due to the slick and icy road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer in Waynesboro measured 3.9 inches of snow. Heavy snow across Wayne County resulted in difficult driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The start of a new year brought slight drought improvements to Middle Tennessee in January 2025. A significant winter storm in the middle of the month brought an average of 3 to 6 inches of snow. Its liquid equivalent combined with additional rain throughout the month meant Middle Tennessee received 3 to 7 inches of liquid precipitation. Unfortunately, the higher liquid totals generally favored the northern half of Middle TN and not the worst of the drought are across the south. Still, this did lead to drought improvement with about 30 percent of the area in at least severe drought. About 6 percent of this area was classified as extreme drought which was an improvement from 12 percent in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from December 2024 improved in January 2025 with severe drought conditions across Giles County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air and moisture advection into Middle Tennessee on February 6, 2025, helped to set the stage for thunderstorms to develop across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. These thunderstorms tended to track eastward along a boundary that had set up near the Tennessee and Kentucky border. One particular thunderstorm intensified rapidly as it tracked eastward towards the Cumberland Plateau. The thunderstorm interacting with the boundary combined with the terrain of the Cumberland Plateau likely allowed it to begin rotating and become tornadic. This one thunderstorm cycled several times and produced four tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inflow from a tornadic thunderstorm caused trees to uproot and snap along Ben Mason Road. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe weather and flooding event unfolded across Middle Tennessee on February 15-16, 2025. As well above average moisture spread into the area, this helped set the stage for very heavy rainfall across Middle Tennessee. Showers and thunderstorms began early during the day on February 15th, and the primary threat with this first round was flooding. The most significant flooding was reported in Montgomery and Robertson counties where numerous roadways were flooded, and several homes were inundated with water. Area rivers and creeks also rose in response to the heavy rain with the Cumberland River, Red River, and Harpeth River all entering flood stage. A second round of showers and thunderstorms moved into Middle Tennessee during the evening of February 15th and through the morning hours of February 16th ahead of a cold front. Additional heavy rain and flash flooding was a threat with these thunderstorms as well, but the risk for severe weather also increased with reports of large hail and high winds received. In the wake of the cold front, non-convective strong winds were observed across much of the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern during the month of February 2025 continued to improve drought conditions across Middle Tennessee. Multiple rain events, including a significant flooding event during the middle of the month, brought an average of 6 to 12 inches of rain to the area. This led to only about 6 percent of Middle Tennessee in severe drought which was an improvement over 30 percent in January. Additionally, none of the area was in extreme drought which again was an improvement from 6 percent the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from January 2025 improved to abnormally dry to moderate drought across Fentress County in February.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 15th pushed the Clinch river near Tazewell into flood stage by the early morning hours on February 16th. It crested in moderate flood stage on the 17th, but didn't fall back below flood stage until the afternoon of February 18th. The crest of 18.9 at the stream gage on 02/17/2025 left flood debris on low spots of Riverside Road downstream from Hwy 25E. Thus the stage there is affected by the river and the pools from Norris Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe weather and flooding event unfolded across Middle Tennessee on February 15-16, 2025. As well above average moisture spread into the area, this helped set the stage for very heavy rainfall across Middle Tennessee. Showers and thunderstorms began early during the day on February 15th, and the primary threat with this first round was flooding. The most significant flooding was reported in Montgomery and Robertson counties where numerous roadways were flooded, and several homes were inundated with water. Area rivers and creeks also rose in response to the heavy rain with the Cumberland River, Red River, and Harpeth River all entering flood stage. A second round of showers and thunderstorms moved into Middle Tennessee during the evening of February 15th and through the morning hours of February 16th ahead of a cold front. Additional heavy rain and flash flooding was a threat with these thunderstorms as well, but the risk for severe weather also increased with reports of large hail and high winds received. In the wake of the cold front, non-convective strong winds were observed across much of the area. While these winds were generally less than 58 mph, the Crossville ASOS station did observe a brief gust up to 60 mph which caused minor damage in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Sycamore Creek in Cheatham County to overflow out of its bank. No homes along the creek were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air and moisture advection into Middle Tennessee on February 6, 2025, helped to set the stage for thunderstorms to develop across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. These thunderstorms tended to track eastward along a boundary that had set up near the Tennessee and Kentucky border. One particular thunderstorm intensified rapidly as it tracked eastward towards the Cumberland Plateau. The thunderstorm interacting with the boundary combined with the terrain of the Cumberland Plateau likely allowed it to begin rotating and become tornadic. This one thunderstorm cycled several times and produced four tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few houses just north of Clarkrange received hail the evening of February 6th. A few minutes of hail was reported, leading to roofs being damaged as well as some vehicle damage. One resident reported hail of nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm front lifted north into Kentucky and severe thunderstorms, including discrete supercells, moved ahead of the cold front into East Tennessee, producing multiple hazards. Two tornadoes occurred in this event, an unusual occurrence for February in East Tennessee. The Morgan County tornado was the first confirmed February tornado in the county and the strongest February tornado in our area since 1993. The 2nd tornado occurred in Grainger County and was only the 4th confirmed tornado in recorded history in Grainger. These tornadoes were the first February tornadoes in our area since 2011.

A major severe weather and flooding event unfolded across Middle Tennessee on February 15-16, 2025. As well above average moisture spread into the area, this helped set the stage for very heavy rainfall across Middle Tennessee. Showers and thunderstorms began early during the day on February 15th, and the primary threat with this first round was flooding. The most significant flooding was reported in Montgomery and Robertson counties where numerous roadways were flooded, and several homes were inundated with water. Area rivers and creeks also rose in response to the heavy rain with the Cumberland River, Red River, and Harpeth River all entering flood stage. A second round of showers and thunderstorms moved into Middle Tennessee during the evening of February 15th and through the morning hours of February 16th ahead of a cold front. Additional heavy rain and flash flooding was a threat with these thunderstorms as well, but the risk for severe weather also increased with reports of large hail and high winds received. In the wake of the cold front, non-convective strong winds were observed across much of the area. While these winds were generally less than 58 mph, the Crossville ASOS station did observe a brief gust up to 60 mph which caused minor damage in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Humphreys County caused area roadways near and along rivers and creeks to flood. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern during the month of February 2025 continued to improve drought conditions across Middle Tennessee. Multiple rain events, including a significant flooding event during the middle of the month, brought an average of 6 to 12 inches of rain to the area. This led to only about 6 percent of Middle Tennessee in severe drought which was an improvement over 30 percent in January. Additionally, none of the area was in extreme drought which again was an improvement from 6 percent the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from January 2025 improved to abnormally dry to moderate drought across Giles County in February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a major severe weather and flooding event, a potent cold front swept through Middle Tennessee which dropped temperatures significantly starting on February 18, 2025. This much colder air helped to set the stage for snow as another storm system arrived. The highest snow totals were received across northwest Middle Tennessee where 3 to 5 inches of snow was measured. Southern Middle Tennessee generally received less than 3 inches of snow through the morning hours of February 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals near Dover generally ranged from 3 to 4 inches. A CoCoRaHS observer northeast of Dover measured 4.4 inches of snow.

This led to only about 6 percent of Middle Tennessee in severe drought which was an improvement over 30 percent in January. Additionally, none of the area was in extreme drought which again was an improvement from 6 percent the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from January 2025 improved to abnormally dry to moderate drought across Van Buren County in February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 20, 2025. The first round of thunderstorms was a decaying mesoscale convective system (MCS) that produced damaging winds which downed a few trees. The next round was discrete supercells which produced large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes. The largest hail reported during this event was in Williamson County where hailstones of two to three inches fell. Five tornadoes were confirmed during this event with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Cumberland County. The final round of thunderstorms was a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) which produced additional damaging winds before all activity finally ended late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong size hail was reported from a tornado-warned thunderstorm at S James Campbell Boulevard and Brooksmeade in Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of May 8, 2025. With a very cold airmass aloft, this allowed for steep lapse rates which helped to set the stage for large hail. Several reports of large hail was received across Middle Tennessee with the largest hail of two inches being reported in Franklin. Additionally, severe winds also downed several trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated quarter size hail was reported south of Cumberland Furnace.

Significant instability built across the Mid-South as temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and lower 80s and dewpoints topped out in the mid to upper 60s. This warm moist air provided very high instability for this time of year. Additionally, plentiful shear and exceptional lapse rates were available. Thunderstorms were able to grow upscale quickly across West Tennessee as discrete cells. The discrete cells eventually congealed into a line later in the evening. Damaging winds and hail were the primary threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just below golf ball sized hail, east of Gadsden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 20, 2025. The first round of thunderstorms was a decaying mesoscale convective system (MCS) that produced damaging winds which downed a few trees. The next round was discrete supercells which produced large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes. The largest hail reported during this event was in Williamson County where hailstones of two to three inches fell. Five tornadoes were confirmed during this event with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Cumberland County. The final round of thunderstorms was a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) which produced additional damaging winds before all activity finally ended late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail was reported near Elkins Road in Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 20, 2025. The first round of thunderstorms was a decaying mesoscale convective system (MCS) that produced damaging winds which downed a few trees. The next round was discrete supercells which produced large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes. The largest hail reported during this event was in Williamson County where hailstones of two to three inches fell. Five tornadoes were confirmed during this event with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Cumberland County. The final round of thunderstorms was a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) which produced additional damaging winds before all activity finally ended late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported near Thompson's Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability built across the Mid-South as temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and lower 80s and dewpoints topped out in the mid to upper 60s. This warm moist air provided very high instability for this time of year. Additionally, plentiful shear and exceptional lapse rates were available. Thunderstorms were able to grow upscale quickly across West Tennessee as discrete cells. The discrete cells eventually congealed into a line later in the evening. Damaging winds and hail were the primary threats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over Middle Tennessee during the afternoon of May 19, 2025. These thunderstorms were generally non-severe, but did produce very strong winds in the Nashville metro area with trees reported down as well as power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 20, 2025. The first round of thunderstorms was a decaying mesoscale convective system (MCS) that produced damaging winds which downed a few trees. The next round was discrete supercells which produced large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes. The largest hail reported during this event was in Williamson County where hailstones of two to three inches fell. Five tornadoes were confirmed during this event with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Cumberland County. The final round of thunderstorms was a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) which produced additional damaging winds before all activity finally ended late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong size hail was reported north of Spring Hill.

A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down, a home sustained damage and power outages near Springville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability built across the Mid-South as temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and lower 80s and dewpoints topped out in the mid to upper 60s. This warm moist air provided very high instability for this time of year. Additionally, plentiful shear and exceptional lapse rates were available. Thunderstorms were able to grow upscale quickly across West Tennessee as discrete cells. The discrete cells eventually congealed into a line later in the evening. Damaging winds and hail were the primary threats.

However, potential energy in the atmosphere was lacking, with severe storms only generating a handful of damage reports and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 dispatch center reported that Windrock road was closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emory river near Oakdale went into flood for close to 7 hours during the morning of March 16th. It crossed into minor flood stage during the early morning hours, crested in moderate flood at 28.71 shortly after daybreak, then fell back below flood stage before noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of thunderstorms and severe weather to Middle Tennessee on March 15, 2025. The main risk for severe weather was associated with a line of thunderstorms that tracks east across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours. This line produced one EF-1 tornado near Christiana. As this line moved towards the Cumberland Plateau, it strengthened and caused wind damage in numerous locations. Some of the worst wind damage was found in Palmer where an estimated swath of 95 mph winds uprooted trees and caused structural damage. Numerous downed trees and structural damage was also found in White, Fentress, and Overton counties. These multiple rounds of rain also caused flooding issues with flash flooding and reports of road closures reported across Middle Tennessee. This heavy rain also caused several rivers to rise into flood stage for days after this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding reported in Cannon County by Emergency Management and Social Media/Cannon Severe Weather. Many roads were flooded or washed out, beginning around the 11 pm CDT hour, with the second round of heavy rainfall. Areas of worst flooding included Woodbury, Readyville, and Auburntown. Flood was not quite as bad as the May 8-9, 2024 flood but was similar in scope. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex moved across the Lower Mississippi Valley during the early morning hours. A second area of convection developed over Central Arkansas and began to approach the Mid-South during the late morning to early afternoon. Surface-based instability flourished across the region along with supportive shear and steep lapse rates, which resulted in damaging winds and large hail across West Tennessee.

Damaging wind reports were seen throughout the Cumberland plateau and Tennessee valley, but the strongest storms and greatest concentration of severe weather reports were mainly in the southern Tennessee valley. Flash flooding, damaging winds, and even some severe sized hail were seen there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms covered Arkansas during the early morning hours on May 20, 2025. As these storms moved toward the Mid-South, the cap weakened, and one segment near the Tennessee/Mississippi border intensified and produced wind damage. These storms exited the region by mid-morning.||A brief lull in thunderstorm activity occurred from late morning until early afternoon. Gusty southwest winds aided in rapid atmospheric recovery with SBCAPE values greater than 2000 J/kg by mid-afternoon. In addition, dewpoints remained in the low 70s with bulk shear around 50 kts. Initially, storms were supercellular in nature and eventually formed into a line during the evening hours. Damaging winds and hail were common during this event, and a weak tornado occurred near Jackson, TN.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down in a wooded area just south of James Lawrence Road, to the west of Smith Lane in far southwest Jackson. As the tornado moved northeast, damage to a warehouse building occurred on Smith Lane. Multiple videos showed some roof debris being lofted into the air from the warehouse. The tornado lifted as it moved onto McKellar-Sipes Regional Airport, with some of the debris from the warehouse deposited onto the airfield. Peak winds were estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level jet moved across the region during the overnight hours, enhancing lift and weakening the capping inversion. Robust development occurred overnight in a very unstable airmass with plentiful shear. These storms moved east of the region by mid-morning.||The airmass was able to quickly recover as warm, moist air, quickly advected into the region. A cold front moved into the region, triggering storms ahead of the front during the late afternoon. Plentiful shear, helicity, and steep lapse rates supported severe storms that produced all hazards.

These storms moved east of the region by mid-morning.||The airmass was able to quickly recover as warm, moist air, quickly advected into the region. A cold front moved into the region, triggering storms ahead of the front during the late afternoon. Plentiful shear, helicity, and steep lapse rates supported severe storms that produced all hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree is down near Old Paris Murray Rd and Chapel Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary progressed across Tennessee and further into Alabama and Georgia during the mid day and evening hours. Weak but supportive shear combined with instability helped power a mesoscale convective system that brushed Marion and Hamilton counties as the complex exited Tennessee, leading to a couple instances of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and powerlines were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms covered Arkansas during the early morning hours on May 20, 2025. As these storms moved toward the Mid-South, the cap weakened, and one segment near the Tennessee/Mississippi border intensified and produced wind damage. These storms exited the region by mid-morning.||A brief lull in thunderstorm activity occurred from late morning until early afternoon. Gusty southwest winds aided in rapid atmospheric recovery with SBCAPE values greater than 2000 J/kg by mid-afternoon. In addition, dewpoints remained in the low 70s with bulk shear around 50 kts. Initially, storms were supercellular in nature and eventually formed into a line during the evening hours. Damaging winds and hail were common during this event, and a weak tornado occurred near Jackson, TN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down at the intersection of Old Memphis Rd and Fayne Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex moved across the Lower Mississippi Valley during the early morning hours. A second area of convection developed over Central Arkansas and began to approach the Mid-South during the late morning to early afternoon. Surface-based instability flourished across the region along with supportive shear and steep lapse rates, which resulted in damaging winds and large hail across West Tennessee.

A shortwave trough pushing a weak cold front through the area served as the forcing for this event. The only severe weather observed was a brief EF-0 tornado accompanied by severe rear-flank downdraft winds that injured several residents. This event was unusual: while effective shear was strong at 40 to 50 knots, CAPE was very limited at less than 500J total, and lapse rates were really weak below 6C from the surface upwards. The parent thunderstorm was only broadly and very weakly rotating at time of tornadogenesis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in southern Loudon County in the early morning hours with max winds of 85 mph. Tornado was in Loudon County for roughly 1 mile before exiting into northern Monroe County east of Holt Road. Several injuries occurred when either the tornado itself or the rear flank downdraft winds rolled a camper RV several times up and over a wooden service post. In addition to the RV on the south side of the damage swath, several trees suffered damage, there was roof damage to small structures and a barn, and the tornado left cycloidal markings in the field beyond the RV and houses. When the tornado reached the tree line at the edge of Loudon, several trees had large branches broken off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado entered Monroe County from Loudon County and remained on the ground for approximately another mile through northern portions of Monroe County, inflicting minor roof damage to several farm buildings, minor tree damage, and causing additional injuries upon rolling a second RV camper vehicle. The tornado eventually crossed New Hope Road and dissipated shortly thereafter.

The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees damaged along Hwy 371.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over the Central Plains moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley during the evening hours of Friday, March 14, 2025. Increasing moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front lifted dewpoints into the low to mid 60s. This coupled with increasing height falls and a mid-level 80 knot jet rotating around the main upper low supported severe thunderstorm development. Large looping hodographs, MLCAPE values over 2000 J/kg, 60 knots of bulk shear, and 0-1 km SRH values between 250 and 350 m2/s2 supported an all hazards threat. Storm mode during the overnight hours was supercellular with large hail, damaging winds and several tornadoes, a few of which were strong (EF2+), mainly across northeast Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel and northeast Mississippi. ||The main upper low lifted into the western Great Lakes Region by early Saturday morning, March 15, 2025. The cold front stalled just to the west of the Mississippi River. Simultaneously, a neutrally-tilted longwave trough moved across the Southern Plains and pushed a 100 knot mid-level jet into the region. This placed West Tennessee and  north Mississippi beneath the left exit region of the jet with widespread lift and diffluence available. As a result, showers and thunderstorms rapidly redeveloped across north Mississippi and parts of West Tennessee Saturday morning. Storms quickly formed into a line that slowly moved east. The line of storms produced damaging winds, large hail and a weak tornado Saturday morning. The airmass ahead of the line became even more favorable for severe storms by Saturday afternoon with MLCAPE values increasing to 1500 J/kg and 0-1km SRH values reaching 300 m2/s2 as a surface low moving along the cold front and backed surface winds across northeast Mississippi.  Discrete supercells formed ahead of the line during the afternoon producing a couple of weak tornadoes and damaging winds over parts of northeast Missisisppi. Flash flooding was also common due to the slow moving nature of the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported across Lexington. Several roads were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dewpoints of 70 degrees played a significant role in reducing convective inhibition across the Mid-South, as well as enhancing low-level CAPE for storm initiation. 0-6km shear was not particularly strong, generally less than 25 knots, but increased to around 35 kts with a passing shortwave. Storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down in Cedar Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of strong to severe thunderstorms originating in the southern Plains pushed all the way through the mid South and Tennessee Valley on the 7th. The storms produced numerous reports of wind damage in southern middle Tennessee and north Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down across the roadway on Elkton Pike. Time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Concord Road, about five miles northeast of Ripley. Large tree limbs were downed and a small barn was damaged. The tornado began to track northeast along Concord Road downing and uprooting additional trees. A barn along the road lost a portion of its wooden roof. Uprooted trees and snapped tree trunks were noted as the tornado tracked toward Wilkes Road. On Wilkes Road, an outbuilding lost a portion of its metal roof panels. Tree damage continued along the path as it neared the southeast side of Gates. A home on Mitchell Road lost its siding and tree damage continued to W Wardlow Street. The tornado lifted shortly after. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms roared east-northeast through southern middle Tennessee during the late evening of the 5th to just after Midnight on the 6th. An EF-1 tracked nearly 9 miles in Franklin County from Cowan to southeast of Sewanee.  Thunderstorm winds produced several areas of damage in Lincoln County as well. Also, flash flooding resulted in multiple water rescues due to excessive rainfall in a short period of time.||Flash flooding Lincoln County CAD reports in Q drive.||Flash flooding reports in Franklin County CAD report in Q drive (pdf).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Cowan on Franklin Street, causing several uprooted and split trees before continuing eastward. As the tornado continued just south along Sewanee Highway, sporadic tree damage was observed. It peaked in intensity along Saint Marys Lane where several large hardwood trees were uprooted and split. Structural damage to the roof of a brick shelter was also noted at this location due to the uprooting of the tree that was directly above it. The track continued southeastward across Sherwood Road before a secondary large area of damage was observed along Rattlesnake Spring Road. At this point, multiple hardwood trees were uprooted and snapped in multiple directions. The tornado continued south-southeast towards Gudger Road, following along the ridgeline uprooting another tree before lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms underneath a suppressive ridge occurred during the PM hours. One storm produced severe straight line wind damage in Morgan County, Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts, with one tree landing  on a car. A nearby barn suffered the loss of its roof to the high storm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front coupled with a highly saturated atmosphere led to numerous thunderstorms across the area. One storm became severe, producing severe wind damage and a house fire sparked by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts blew down large tree branches, bringing powerlines down and causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered air mass thunderstorms developed over the east Tennessee mountains, Cumberland plateau, and central Tennessee valley during the afternoon and evening hours of August 22nd. These storms produced some minor flooding near the Karns and Solway areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of water coming into a house near Hardin Valley Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. The slow moving nature of these storms resulted in heavy rains and notable flooding near the I-26 corridor between Kingsport and Johnson City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos from social media showed water coming into the first level of a house near Cedar Valley Blvd, likely due to fast rises on nearby South Fork Cedar Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered air mass thunderstorms developed over the east Tennessee mountains, Cumberland plateau, and central Tennessee valley during the afternoon and evening hours of August 22nd. These storms produced some minor flooding near the Karns and Solway areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of flooding on Pellissippi Parkway near Solway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadways are flooded across Dresden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell developed during the mid afternoon hours, producing large hail in Winchester in Franklin County.  A secondary batch of thunderstorms produced a swath damaging winds across southwest through south central Lincoln County during the evening hours.  Several trees and power lines were knocked down along with metal roofing from a barn at one location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to half dollar dized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms roared east-northeast through southern middle Tennessee during the late evening of the 5th to just after Midnight on the 6th. An EF-1 tracked nearly 9 miles in Franklin County from Cowan to southeast of Sewanee.  Thunderstorm winds produced several areas of damage in Lincoln County as well. Also, flash flooding resulted in multiple water rescues due to excessive rainfall in a short period of time.||Flash flooding Lincoln County CAD reports in Q drive.||Flash flooding reports in Franklin County CAD report in Q drive (pdf).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was was knocked down on Thorogood Street near Stewart Street. Time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down just south of Mount Moriah Church Road, south of the town of Friendship during the early morning hours of April 5th. The tornado caused some minor tree damage along this road, before continuing northward along East Road. Just south of Highway 412, additional tree damage was observed. The tornado continued northward just to the east of the town of Friendship, causing a tree to be uprooted along Friendship Eaton Road. The tornado lifted shortly after. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of strong to severe thunderstorms originating in the southern Plains pushed all the way through the mid South and Tennessee Valley on the 7th. The storms produced numerous reports of wind damage in southern middle Tennessee and north Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were knocked down on Old Lincoln Road. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system strengthened as it moved across southern Missouri into western Kentucky thanks to surface heating and increasing instability. This system produced an outflow boundary, causing damaging winds across parts of West Tennessee. The mesoscale convective system eventually sagged into Henry County, TN, resulting in large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down across Old Springville Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper level trough combined with unusually low freezing heights, steep lapse rates, and enough CAPE to generate numerous reports of large hail during severe afternoon thunderstorms. Many reports, especially from the Knoxville metro, indicated the hail was a weird soft slush upon impact, with textures closer to graupel stones embedded in a snow mix, not a true ringed hailstone.

A mid to upper level trough combined with unusually low freezing heights, steep lapse rates, and enough CAPE to generate numerous reports of large hail during severe afternoon thunderstorms. Many reports, especially from the Knoxville metro, indicated the hail was a weird soft slush upon impact, with textures closer to graupel stones embedded in a snow mix, not a true ringed hailstone.

A major and historic multi-day severe weather event unfolded across Middle Tennessee during early April 2025. This event began during the afternoon hours of April 2nd and continued through April 6th. These first couple rounds of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail along with a tornado risk. The corridor from southwest Middle Tennessee into the Nashville metro area was hit particularly hard with multiple supercells training for several hours. Large hail was reported, particularly with the early thunderstorms, with hail sizes nearing three inches. A couple of tornadoes were also confirmed with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Wilson County. A brief break was received on April 4th, but the event ended with a potent line of thunderstorms sweeping east through the area April 5th and into the early morning hours of April 6th. This final round of thunderstorms brought a wide swath of damaging winds to several Middle Tennessee counties, including Perry, Humphreys, Dickson, Cheatham, Davidson, Sumner, Wilson, Macon, and Trousdale counties. Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across Houston County caused flash flooding, especially along waterways and within low-lying areas. By the end of this event, observations and radar estimates showed 8 to 10 inches of rain had fallen across Houston County. Extent of damages is unknown.

Many reports, especially from the Knoxville metro, indicated the hail was a weird soft slush upon impact, with textures closer to graupel stones embedded in a snow mix, not a true ringed hailstone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and plenty of instability helped to set the stage for a summer-like afternoon across Middle Tennessee on April 23, 2025. Scattered thunderstorms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. With little forcing and wind shear, these thunderstorms were generally non-severe. However, slow storm motions meant that these nearly stationary thunderstorms were able to produce flash flooding in a few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain near Woodbury caused flash flooding along Hollis Creek Road near Sunny Slope Road as nearby Hollis Creek overfilled its banks. Water flowing over the road became as high as four feet deep. Extent of damages is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major and historic multi-day severe weather event unfolded across Middle Tennessee during early April 2025. This event began during the afternoon hours of April 2nd and continued through April 6th. These first couple rounds of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail along with a tornado risk. The corridor from southwest Middle Tennessee into the Nashville metro area was hit particularly hard with multiple supercells training for several hours. Large hail was reported, particularly with the early thunderstorms, with hail sizes nearing three inches. A couple of tornadoes were also confirmed with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Wilson County. A brief break was received on April 4th, but the event ended with a potent line of thunderstorms sweeping east through the area April 5th and into the early morning hours of April 6th. This final round of thunderstorms brought a wide swath of damaging winds to several Middle Tennessee counties, including Perry, Humphreys, Dickson, Cheatham, Davidson, Sumner, Wilson, Macon, and Trousdale counties. Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harpeth River at Kingston Springs crested to 26.8 feet which was minor flood stage. At this stage, residences on Zapata Drive, Sawyer Farms Lane, Bluff View Drive, and Riverview Drive were impacted by flooding as well as U.S. Highway 70 near Highway 249, Walkup Road near Sawyer Farms Lane, Riverview Drive near Elkmore Drive, and Cedar Hill Road near Griffintown Road. Water also approached West Kingston Springs Road at Big Turnbull Creek.

This final round of thunderstorms brought a wide swath of damaging winds to several Middle Tennessee counties, including Perry, Humphreys, Dickson, Cheatham, Davidson, Sumner, Wilson, Macon, and Trousdale counties. Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cumberland River at Dover crested to 67.34 feet which was minor flood stage. At this stage, water impacted Bellwood Brance Road, Cross Creek Road, Frank Earhart Road near Mud Hill Road, and River Road west of Highway 79. Water also impacted boat launch areas on Colson Road, Fitzhughs Landing Road, Forrest Street, and Dyer Creek Road. Near Cumberland City and Indian Mound, water continued to impact properties on Atkinson Drive, TN-149, Wildcat Creek Road east of Highway 46, and the ferry crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep synoptic trough over the Rockies with a strengthening surface low over the Plains led to a tightening of the pressure gradient over the Mid-South. With the low level jet increasing in magnitude, the typical mountain wind locations recorded a couple of high wind non-thunderstorm gusts, and one severe thunderstorm produced a brief pocket of severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High non-thunderstorm wind gusts stripped a few shingles from a four year old roof on a home in South Central, TN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado formed near the Hatchie River in southern Lauderdale County. The tornado moved quickly to the northeast and produced mainly tree damage as it approached the Cherry community near Ft. Pillow. The tornado moved through the Cherry community, damaged a barn, and uprooted trees along Highway 371. In addition, the doors were blown out of a church across the street from the barn on Highway 371. The tornado tracked northeastward across a field to Pipkin Rd causing additional tree damage before dissipating in a wooded area north of Highway 87. This survey was aided by the presence of a Tornado Debris Signature and analysis of Sentinel-2 satellite data. Peak winds were estimated at 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours of April 10, 2025. With plenty of instability and wind shear, the ingredients were there for these thunderstorms to strengthen. The main hazard with these thunderstorms was damaging winds with numerous reports of downed trees received. A few reports of large hail were also received with the largest being golf ball size hail near Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds snapped a large tree on Kimpalong Drive. Damages are a rough estimate.
