EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With abundant moisture and instability, the ingredients were there for another summer-like day across Middle Tennessee with scattered convection during the afternoon and evening of April 25, 2025. With little wind shear and forcing, these thunderstorms were non-severe. However, slow storm motions allowed for flash flooding to be the primary risk, especially in Lawrence County where a thunderstorm produced 2 to 4 inches in a short time, causing flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2 to 4 inches in Loretto caused flash flooding across Lambs Ferry Road, likely caused by heavy rainfall rates causing the nearby Bluewater Creek to overfill its banks. Extent of damages is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With abundant moisture and instability, the ingredients were there for another summer-like day across Middle Tennessee with scattered convection during the afternoon and evening of April 25, 2025. With little wind shear and forcing, these thunderstorms were non-severe. However, slow storm motions allowed for flash flooding to be the primary risk, especially in Lawrence County where a thunderstorm produced 2 to 4 inches in a short time, causing flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2 to 4 inches near St. Joseph caused flash flooding along Fairview Road east of South Old Miliary Road with a tree and mud covering part of the roadway. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major and historic multi-day severe weather event unfolded across Middle Tennessee during early April 2025. This event began during the afternoon hours of April 2nd and continued through April 6th. These first couple rounds of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail along with a tornado risk. The corridor from southwest Middle Tennessee into the Nashville metro area was hit particularly hard with multiple supercells training for several hours. Large hail was reported, particularly with the early thunderstorms, with hail sizes nearing three inches. A couple of tornadoes were also confirmed with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Wilson County. A brief break was received on April 4th, but the event ended with a potent line of thunderstorms sweeping east through the area April 5th and into the early morning hours of April 6th. This final round of thunderstorms brought a wide swath of damaging winds to several Middle Tennessee counties, including Perry, Humphreys, Dickson, Cheatham, Davidson, Sumner, Wilson, Macon, and Trousdale counties. Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buffalo River near Lobelville crested to 17.39 feet which was minor flood stage. At this stage, flooding impacted agricultural land and properties along the river, including Dailey Loop, Caruthers Trace Lane, Lost Creek Road near Gilmer Bridge Road, and portions of the Buffalo River Country Club. Water covered Old State Highway 13 south of School House Road and Gilmer Bridge Road. Water also impacted waterfront properties off East 8th Avenue in Leeper Bottom and approached Cuba Landing Road and Squeeze Bottom Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the southern Appalachian region on April 6th, producing several hours of moderate to heavy rainfall in the mountains of east Tennessee and western North Carolina. Runoff from this event resulted in the Pigeon River at Newport going into minor flood stage from late in the evening on April 7th and lasting into the early morning hours on April 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains in western North Carolina pushed the Pigeon River at Newport into minor flood stage for around 5 hours, beginning at 8:30 PM EST on April 7th and ending at 1:15 AM EST on April 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours of April 10, 2025. With plenty of instability and wind shear, the ingredients were there for these thunderstorms to strengthen. The main hazard with these thunderstorms was damaging winds with numerous reports of downed trees received. A few reports of large hail were also received with the largest being golf ball size hail near Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed trees and powerlines near Vernon Winfrey Avenue in Nashville. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree landed on a powerline at Hwy 641 and Big Sandy River Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours of April 10, 2025. With plenty of instability and wind shear, the ingredients were there for these thunderstorms to strengthen. The main hazard with these thunderstorms was damaging winds with numerous reports of downed trees received. A few reports of large hail were also received with the largest being golf ball size hail near Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nashville International Airport ASOS measured a 61 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours of April 10, 2025. With plenty of instability and wind shear, the ingredients were there for these thunderstorms to strengthen. The main hazard with these thunderstorms was damaging winds with numerous reports of downed trees received. A few reports of large hail were also received with the largest being golf ball size hail near Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds caused minor roof damage to Thousand Hills Cowboy Church in Ethridge. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down in Wilson County. This tornado began near Vesta Road just east of the Nashville Superspeedway, causing timber damage and minor structural damage. The tornado continued northeast, causing additional damage near Flatwoods Road and Murfreesboro Road. Some homes sustained minor damage, including fences and adjacent trees snapped. The tornado then crossed E Richmond Shop Road, continuing damage to trees and structures. Continuing northeast, the tornado crossed over Cedar Forest Road where notable timber damage was found using satellite imagery. Here, swaths of hardwoods were snapped and uprooted near Steeplechase Drive. Some homes sustained minor roof damage. The tornado continued northeast, crossing Chicken Road and SE Tater Peeler Road, continuing damage to trees with at least minor structure damage. The tornado continued northeast, crossing Cainsville Road and Sparta Pike, causing additional timber damage and damaging some farm structures. The tornado then dissipated near Shop Springs Road. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major and historic multi-day severe weather event unfolded across Middle Tennessee during early April 2025. This event began during the afternoon hours of April 2nd and continued through April 6th. These first couple rounds of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail along with a tornado risk. The corridor from southwest Middle Tennessee into the Nashville metro area was hit particularly hard with multiple supercells training for several hours. Large hail was reported, particularly with the early thunderstorms, with hail sizes nearing three inches. A couple of tornadoes were also confirmed with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Wilson County. A brief break was received on April 4th, but the event ended with a potent line of thunderstorms sweeping east through the area April 5th and into the early morning hours of April 6th. This final round of thunderstorms brought a wide swath of damaging winds to several Middle Tennessee counties, including Perry, Humphreys, Dickson, Cheatham, Davidson, Sumner, Wilson, Macon, and Trousdale counties. Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds caused major roof damage to buildings along West Main Street in Hendersonville. Metal siding was reported to be piled up in front of Red Rooster Cafe. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep synoptic trough over the Rockies with a strengthening surface low over the Plains led to a tightening of the pressure gradient over the Mid-South. With the low level jet increasing in magnitude, the typical mountain wind locations recorded a couple of high wind non-thunderstorm gusts, and one severe thunderstorm produced a brief pocket of severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed trees and powerlines along Highway 109 in Laguardo. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep synoptic trough over the Rockies with a strengthening surface low over the Plains led to a tightening of the pressure gradient over the Mid-South. With the low level jet increasing in magnitude, the typical mountain wind locations recorded a couple of high wind non-thunderstorm gusts, and one severe thunderstorm produced a brief pocket of severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 11 AM EDT and 3 PM EDT, the Cove Mountain Fire Tower wind gauge recorded several high wind qualifying gusts, with the maximum occurring at the 11 AM EDT observation of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front to the west of East Tennessee with a near-record (for this portion of the calendar year) upper ridge to the east set the stage for only isolated convection in East Tennessee. Strongest storm of the day produced severe hail near Oneida.

A mid to upper level trough combined with unusually low freezing heights, steep lapse rates, and enough CAPE to generate numerous reports of large hail during severe afternoon thunderstorms. Many reports, especially from the Knoxville metro, indicated the hail was a weird soft slush upon impact, with textures closer to graupel stones embedded in a snow mix, not a true ringed hailstone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado formed over northern Monteagle, first producing damage along Wrens Nest Road. The tornado moved east into Summerfield and continued along Highway 41 into Tracy City. The tornado then strengthened as it entered into Tracy City with significant tree damage observed along the path as well as some damage to structures noted at the tornado's peak. The stands of a ball field in Tracy City had its metal roofing completely removed and blown into various areas of the park. Other structure damage noted was a roof blown completely off of a home as well as numerous homes with roof damage caused by fallen trees. There was also damage to several farm outbuildings, some of which lost their roofs or were collapsed. The tornado lifted south of Tracy City with the final damage observed near King Road. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major and historic multi-day severe weather event unfolded across Middle Tennessee during early April 2025. This event began during the afternoon hours of April 2nd and continued through April 6th. These first couple rounds of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail along with a tornado risk. The corridor from southwest Middle Tennessee into the Nashville metro area was hit particularly hard with multiple supercells training for several hours. Large hail was reported, particularly with the early thunderstorms, with hail sizes nearing three inches. A couple of tornadoes were also confirmed with the strongest being an EF-1 tornado in Wilson County. A brief break was received on April 4th, but the event ended with a potent line of thunderstorms sweeping east through the area April 5th and into the early morning hours of April 6th. This final round of thunderstorms brought a wide swath of damaging winds to several Middle Tennessee counties, including Perry, Humphreys, Dickson, Cheatham, Davidson, Sumner, Wilson, Macon, and Trousdale counties. Widespread 60-70 mph winds were common, however, embedded microbursts likely produced winds up to 90 mph in some spots. Before this line exited the area, it was responsible for producing one last EF-1 tornado in Grundy County. ||These multiple rounds of thunderstorms were also responsible for widespread flooding and flash flooding. By the end of this event, highest rainfall totals were received across the northwest corner of Middle Tennessee and into the Nashville metro area where observations and radar estimates showed 6 to 12 inches of rain. Fortunately, the brief break in activity on April 4th likely prevented more significant flooding, but widespread reports of road closures and streams and creeks spilling out of banks were still received. River flooding was also a widespread issue with sixteen river gauges reaching minor flood stage and six river gauges reaching moderate flood stage. ||Fortunately, no reports of injuries or fatalities were received over this prolonged severe weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado first began near Interstate 40 near Buffalo, snapping and uprooting numerous trees. The tornado continued northeast along Highway 13, causing additional timber damage and damaging Overholt's Farm Market. It is believed to have dissipated in a heavily wooded area east of Highway 13 thereafter. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system tracked across the eastern United States, steering a very strong low-level jet over Middle Tennessee on March 4, 2025. These very strong upper-level winds were able to mix down to the surface which resulted in damaging winds across the area. The strongest winds during this event were measured across the Cumberland Plateau where wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph occurred. While wind gusts weren't as strong west of the Cumberland Plateau, several hours of strong winds still caused damage. Across Middle Tennessee, numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines along with power outages were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong non-thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated around 60 mph across Van Buren County. Several trees were reported to be downed. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of August 16, 2025. While these thunderstorms were generally non-severe, one thunderstorm did produce a brief 58 mph wind gust at the Crossville Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crossville ASOS measured a 58 mph thunderstorm wind gust. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening of August 17, 2025. These thunderstorms were non-severe, but did produce gusty winds. One thunderstorm near Celina produced minor tree and power pole damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds caused a rotten tree to fall onto a mobile home. A couple of other tree branches fell as well with one branch causing a power pole to lean. No injuries were reported. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved through middle and eastern Tennessee during the afternoon of June 6th, ahead of a developing surface low over far western Tennessee and the Missouri Bootheel region. These storms produced some marginally severe hail, damaging winds, and some isolated flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down by powerful thunderstorm wind gusts across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough and low pressure system helped generate a powerful low level jet of winds that led to both high wind reports and wind damage. However, potential energy in the atmosphere was lacking, with severe storms only generating a handful of damage reports and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down by powerful thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A handful of strong thunderstorms moved north out of Alabama and northern Georgia the afternoon of June 18th, affecting southeast Tennessee and areas in the east Tennessee mountains near the Great Smoky Mountains and Interstate 40. A few thunderstorms trained along the spine of the Smoky Mountains, producing flash flooding and a mudslide that impacted Interstate 40 just east of Hartford. These impacts compounded the damage further east on Interstate 40 inflicted by Hurricane Helene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 40 was closed by flooding and debris near mile marker 451, just west of the North Carolina state line. Multiple vehicles were stranded and water rescues were ongoing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms advanced across Middle Tennessee during the evening of March 19, 2025, ahead of a cold front. The main threat with these thunderstorms was strong winds with numerous reports of downed trees received, especially in and around the Nashville metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed an oak tree that fell across Wilson Pike at New Hope Church in Brentwood. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm in Lawrence County during the evening of August 19, 2025, was the cause of a person being struck by lightning near Ethridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was struck by lightning near Buffalo Road and Flatwoods Road near Ethridge. Fatalities were not reported, but extent of injuries was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late destabilization underneath the still strong upper level ridge led to an isolated severe storm in Roane County. The environmental profile suggested downbursts which was the cause for the damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved through middle and eastern Tennessee during the afternoon of June 6th, ahead of a developing surface low over far western Tennessee and the Missouri Bootheel region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous photos and videos from social media feeds that showed flooded homes and roadways in Alpha area of west Hamblen county. Some homes in the Seven Oaks subdivision had a foot of water in their basements, with similar impacts to nearby subdivisions off of Murrell Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved through middle and eastern Tennessee during the afternoon of June 6th, ahead of a developing surface low over far western Tennessee and the Missouri Bootheel region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts across southern portions of Marion county. Some of these trees fell into powerlines causing multiple power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough crossed the Four Corners region and dipped into northern Mexico on February 14, 2025. In response, deep southwesterly flow set up over the Lower Mississippi Valley and advected Gulf moisture poleward. Showers and thunderstorms blossomed across the region during the early morning hours on February 15, 2025. Meanwhile, a warm front quickly accelerated northward during the morning of February 15th as surface low pressure traversed the Southern Plains. The front stalled across the northern parts of the Mid-South. Heavy rainfall was focused along and north of the stalled front with 4 to 7 inches falling north of Interstate 40. Flash flooding was common across northwest Tennessee into northeast Arkansas during the day on February 15th. The event transitioned toward severe weather during the evening of February 15th as the airmass south of the warm front destabilized. The surface low tracked into north-central Arkansas and a line of severe storms developed and pushed across the Mid-South during the evening hours of February 15th exiting into Middle Tennessee and northern Alabama by midnight. Damaging winds, large hail, and a few weak tornadoes occurred. A levee overtopped and then breached in the Rives, TN area on February 16th, resulting in major flooding in the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail in downtown Paris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell in Loudon, a CoCoRaHS observer in Loudon measured 2.7 inches of snow. The COOP observer for Lenoir City Utilities measured 2.5 inches of snow. Additional reports were 1.8 inches of snow in Lenoir City, 2 inches in Loudon, and 1.8 inches of snow north of Lenoir City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Rhea County, with the public reporting 2 inches of snowfall in Evensville, and 1.3 inches of snow in Spring City. The COOP observer in Spring City measured 1.6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell in Meigs County, a CoCoRaHS observer in Decatur reported 2.5 inches of snow, another in Ten Mile reported 2 inches of snow. The COOP observer in Decatur reported 2 inches of snow and light ice pellets. Finally a member of the public in Ten Mile reported 2.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer in Athens recorded an event total of 3 inches of snow. Otherwise the COOP observer in Athens recorded 1.2 inches of snow in the first bout, 0 in the second. The public reported 2 inches of snow in Athens and 2 inches of snow in Dentville, and a trained spotter reported 2.5 inches of snow in Etowah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of June 16, 2025. With above normal atmospheric moisture values, these thunderstorms were capable of producing very heavy rain. This combined with very slow storm motions led to a flash flooding threat. The heaviest rain recorded was in southern Maury County where radar estimates and gauges in the area measured four to five inches of rain. This caused flash flooding in the Culleoka and McCain areas. Heavy rain also impacted Robertson and Sumner counties which caused additional flooding, mainly along creeks and in low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Robertson County led to flash flooding of low-lying areas across eastern portions of the county. Known roads impacted included Owens Chapel at Keith Road and Owens Chapel at Wilson Store Road. A few reports of cars stranded with water rescues needed were also received. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds downed a tree onto powerlines along Grifford Lane in Bethpage. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms moved through southern Middle Tennessee during the afternoon of June 7, 2025. This line of thunderstorms was generally non-severe, however, as it neared the Cumberland Plateau, it did produce damaging winds. Several reports of downed trees were received across Bedford, Coffee, and Grundy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a large tree which blocked the intersection of Fremont Road and Highway 56 in Coalmont. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of June 16, 2025. With above normal atmospheric moisture values, these thunderstorms were capable of producing very heavy rain. This combined with very slow storm motions led to a flash flooding threat. The heaviest rain recorded was in southern Maury County where radar estimates and gauges in the area measured four to five inches of rain. This caused flash flooding in the Culleoka and McCain areas. Heavy rain also impacted Robertson and Sumner counties which caused additional flooding, mainly along creeks and in low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of four to five inches in the Culleoka and McCain areas caused flash flooding. Pulaski Highway was reported to be impassable along with Stockard Road. Damage to roadways was reported as roads were washed out. Homes along area creeks were in danger of being inundated with water. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm front lifted north into Kentucky and severe thunderstorms, including discrete supercells, moved ahead of the cold front into East Tennessee, producing multiple hazards. Two tornadoes occurred in this event, an unusual occurrence for February in East Tennessee. The Morgan County tornado was the first confirmed February tornado in the county and the strongest February tornado in our area since 1993. The 2nd tornado occurred in Grainger County and was only the 4th confirmed tornado in recorded history in Grainger. These tornadoes were the first February tornadoes in our area since 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began near McAnally Rd and Campground Rd before moving east along Highway 131. Tree damage began sporadically at first, with a few cedar trees split or blown over, tree damage then began to intensify towards the heart of the damage path. There were several barns and outbuildings with roof damage along 131, and numerous snapped trees. A small chicken coop was lofted up and over the pen around the coop, and left upside down in a ditch but with no scratches or visible damage to the coop. The most significant damage was to a large barn on Highway 131 that was completely destroyed. The tornado continued to Clinch River Hardwoods and Thorn Hill Church. At that location a large tanker trailer was overturned, and the church had some light damage to siding and windows. The tornado lifted where trees were snapped on the side of Clinch Mountain near US Highway 25E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through Middle Tennessee during the evening of June 18, 2025. Severe thunderstorm winds were common along the leading edge of this line of thunderstorms with several reports of downed trees received. Two EF-0 tornadoes were confirmed that occurred at approximately the same time. One was near Centerville in Hickman County while the other was north of Lafayette in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a large tree on a property near Highway 70W between McElhiney Road and Lane Road in McEwen. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during the April 2- April 5 event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along the Rutherford Fork Obion River. About a half-dozen homes were affected in the Obion County part of Kenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level low traversed across the upper Midwest and stretched a cold front over eastern Kansas and southern Missouri. Strong frontogenesis, aided by a strong upper level jet, sagged the aforementioned front southeast into the Mid-South. This quick moving system brought a mix of wintry precipitation from Tuesday evening through Wednesday morning. Arctic air was unable to reach the southernmost areas which resulted in lower snow accumulations, but in the north; strong frontal forcing and cold air present resulted in impactful snow accumulations. Less than typical snow-to-liquid ratios prevailed Tuesday evening, but increased during the overnight hours before the upper low traversed east by sunrise Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of snowfall measured across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low and associated cold front were able to tighten the pressure gradient resulting in strong southerly winds. Deep boundary layer mixing occurred and elevated winds were able to mix down across West Tennessee and cause damage in a few communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down on powerlines on Frank Road near Houston Levee Road. KMEM gusted to 53 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low and associated cold front were able to tighten the pressure gradient resulting in strong southerly winds. Deep boundary layer mixing occurred and elevated winds were able to mix down across West Tennessee and cause damage in a few communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down across Lightfoot Luckett Road, blocking both lanes of traffic. KMO4 gusted to 51 mph shortly before.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm front lifted north into Kentucky and severe thunderstorms, including discrete supercells, moved ahead of the cold front into East Tennessee, producing multiple hazards. Two tornadoes occurred in this event, an unusual occurrence for February in East Tennessee. The Morgan County tornado was the first confirmed February tornado in the county and the strongest February tornado in our area since 1993. The 2nd tornado occurred in Grainger County and was only the 4th confirmed tornado in recorded history in Grainger. These tornadoes were the first February tornadoes in our area since 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado first formed in Fentress County before tracking into heavily forested and rugged areas of Morgan County. Aerial imagery from Tennessee Highway Patrol indicated the tornado produced tree damage as it moved through the forests of Morgan County. The tornado continued to moved southeast where it produced EF-2 damage by destroying two manufactured homes, and damaged multiple barns and outbuildings near the intersection of Twin Bridge Rd and Thurman Lavender Rd. At this location is where the two fatalities and two injuries occurred, both fatalities were inside a manufactured house that was completely destroyed by the tornado. At a separate family residence, a dozen chickens were reported killed along with their coop, a tool shed, and a barn destroyed. Thousands of trees were felled within the debris path, with shredded housing strewn amongst the trees. As the tornado continued southeast, it snapped the top of trees consistent with EF-1 damage near the convergence of Highway 62 and Deer Lodge Highway. At this location at least 35 acres of trees were blown down in a fairly straight and non-convergent pattern facing north relative to the damage swath. Minor roof damage was also noted at this intersection, with small outbuildings destroyed. The tornado continued southeast into forested areas before dissipating prior to Highway 27.

The tornado weakened thereafter with only tree branches observed on She Boss Road and Old Trace Road. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level low traversed across the upper Midwest and stretched a cold front over eastern Kansas and southern Missouri. Strong frontogenesis, aided by a strong upper level jet, sagged the aforementioned front southeast into the Mid-South. This quick moving system brought a mix of wintry precipitation from Tuesday evening through Wednesday morning. Arctic air was unable to reach the southernmost areas which resulted in lower snow accumulations, but in the north; strong frontal forcing and cold air present resulted in impactful snow accumulations. Less than typical snow-to-liquid ratios prevailed Tuesday evening, but increased during the overnight hours before the upper low traversed east by sunrise Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around two inch of snowfall measured across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across East Tennessee, during the afternoon and evening hours of June 27th. These storms produced mainly scattered wind damage, but also caused some flash flooding in Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Couple dozen medium to large trees were blown down along Kell Loop Road outside of Dunlap, TN. A small trailer was blown onto its side, off of the hitch. Another small trailer was dragged in the mud a couple feet. Two porch chairs were blown and dropped on a fence. A couple of chickens were killed. Several trees fell on power lines, resulting in a few power outages. One mailbox was separated from its post and blown into a ditch, underneath the powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability built across the Mid-South as temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and lower 80s and dewpoints topped out in the mid to upper 60s. This warm moist air provided very high instability for this time of year. Additionally, plentiful shear and exceptional lapse rates were available. Thunderstorms were able to grow upscale quickly across West Tennessee as discrete cells. The discrete cells eventually congealed into a line later in the evening. Damaging winds and hail were the primary threats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains, categorized by widespread 2 to 3 inch rainfall totals, affected the Morristown forecast area and broader southern Appalachian region on February 11th and 12th. This lead to flooding along some rivers in east Tennessee between February 12th and 14th, during which time some locations reached moderate to major flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 11th and 12th lead to the Sequatchie river briefly reaching minor flood stage the afternoon of February 13th into the early morning hours on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough helped power a 150 knot jet streak, which in turn powered isentropic lift, bringing enough wintry mix to the higher elevations to cause limited impacts. Meanwhile, a temperature gradient across the southern Appalachians fueled a density driven wind event in Camp Creek in Greene County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice existed on vehicles in Roan Mountain. Nearby at Carvers Gap, ice accumulation on the roadway caused a road department salt truck to slide off the road into the ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level low traversed across the upper Midwest and stretched a cold front over eastern Kansas and southern Missouri. Strong frontogenesis, aided by a strong upper level jet, sagged the aforementioned front southeast into the Mid-South. This quick moving system brought a mix of wintry precipitation from Tuesday evening through Wednesday morning. Arctic air was unable to reach the southernmost areas which resulted in lower snow accumulations, but in the north; strong frontal forcing and cold air present resulted in impactful snow accumulations. Less than typical snow-to-liquid ratios prevailed Tuesday evening, but increased during the overnight hours before the upper low traversed east by sunrise Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snowfall measured across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough crossed the Four Corners region and dipped into northern Mexico on February 14, 2025. In response, deep southwesterly flow set up over the Lower Mississippi Valley and advected Gulf moisture poleward. Showers and thunderstorms blossomed across the region during the early morning hours on February 15, 2025. Meanwhile, a warm front quickly accelerated northward during the morning of February 15th as surface low pressure traversed the Southern Plains. The front stalled across the northern parts of the Mid-South. Heavy rainfall was focused along and north of the stalled front with 4 to 7 inches falling north of Interstate 40. Flash flooding was common across northwest Tennessee into northeast Arkansas during the day on February 15th. The event transitioned toward severe weather during the evening of February 15th as the airmass south of the warm front destabilized. The surface low tracked into north-central Arkansas and a line of severe storms developed and pushed across the Mid-South during the evening hours of February 15th exiting into Middle Tennessee and northern Alabama by midnight. Damaging winds, large hail, and a few weak tornadoes occurred. A levee overtopped and then breached in the Rives, TN area on February 16th, resulting in major flooding in the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palestine Road was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability built across the Mid-South as temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and lower 80s and dewpoints topped out in the mid to upper 60s. This warm moist air provided very high instability for this time of year. Additionally, plentiful shear and exceptional lapse rates were available. Thunderstorms were able to grow upscale quickly across West Tennessee as discrete cells. The discrete cells eventually congealed into a line later in the evening. Damaging winds and hail were the primary threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were knocked down on Highway 77 between White Road and Long Rock Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low and potent cold front tracked across the Southern Plains on March 4, 2005. A tight pressure gradient in advance of this large storm system resulted in southwest winds of 25 mph with gusts up to 50 mph. This resulted in some isolated damage across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked down a powerline near Morris Chapel which ignited a grass fire on Small Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during the April 2- April 5 event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in widespread flooding of the Obion River in Bogota. Nearly 100 structures were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of snow led to light snowfall accumulations across Sullivan County. A CoCoRaHS observer recorded 3.5 inches of snow near Oak Grove, another CoCoRaHS observer reported 2 inches of snow in Blountville, the public reported 3 inches of snow near South Holston Dam, 3 inches of snow near Bluff City, a trained spotter reported 2 inches of snow near Kingsport, and another trained spotter reported 2 inches of snow and a light glaze of ice in Colonial Heights.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Johnson County, with 3 inches of snow reported at 2600 feet in Butler, and the CO-OP observer in Mountain City recording 0.6 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado began just east of Laneview Concord Road southeast of Tuckers Corner in Gibson County around 7:01 PM CST on February 15. A tree fell on a home near the corner of Twin Creek Road and Thetford Road. The tornado continued just north of Thetford Road, causing tree damage including multiple uprooted trees and large branches snapped through a slightly wooded area. The tornado crossed Thetford Road, with additional tree damage noted. The tornado appeared to intensify as it crossed Milan Hwy (Hwy 45E), with the most severe damage occurring between Milan Hwy and Mt. Pisgah Road. Several homes were damaged in this area. Multiple homes on Mt. Pisgah Rd had large sections of their roofs removed and were surrounded by significant tree damage and debris. A barn was also destroyed and shifted off the foundation on Mt. Pisgah Rd. Damage mostly in the form of snapped and uprooted trees continued northeastward toward Idlewild Hollyleaf Rd. The tornado led to additional trees snapped along Walnut Grove Road (the last notable damage), before lifting just to the east around 7:11 PM CST. Peak wind estimated at 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of June 6, 2025. Damaging winds were the primary hazard with these thunderstorms with several reports of downed trees received. One thunderstorm near Spring Hill did produce an EF-0 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed several trees across Robertson County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of June 6, 2025. Damaging winds were the primary hazard with these thunderstorms with several reports of downed trees received. One thunderstorm near Spring Hill did produce an EF-0 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed multiple trees in the Woodmont subdivision in La Vergne. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough crossed the Four Corners region and dipped into northern Mexico on February 14, 2025. In response, deep southwesterly flow set up over the Lower Mississippi Valley and advected Gulf moisture poleward. Showers and thunderstorms blossomed across the region during the early morning hours on February 15, 2025. Meanwhile, a warm front quickly accelerated northward during the morning of February 15th as surface low pressure traversed the Southern Plains. The front stalled across the northern parts of the Mid-South. Heavy rainfall was focused along and north of the stalled front with 4 to 7 inches falling north of Interstate 40. Flash flooding was common across northwest Tennessee into northeast Arkansas during the day on February 15th. The event transitioned toward severe weather during the evening of February 15th as the airmass south of the warm front destabilized. The surface low tracked into north-central Arkansas and a line of severe storms developed and pushed across the Mid-South during the evening hours of February 15th exiting into Middle Tennessee and northern Alabama by midnight. Damaging winds, large hail, and a few weak tornadoes occurred. A levee overtopped and then breached in the Rives, TN area on February 16th, resulting in major flooding in the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was uprooted at a residence with minor damage observed to a surrounding outbuilding and home.

A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Scott County, the CO-OP observer in Oneida reported 2 inches of snowfall, along with a member of the public in Oneida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light snow fell in Morgan County, with 0.8 inches of snow reported on the morning of the 19th halfway through the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Union County, with the COOP observer in Maynardville recording 0.5 inch of snow in each bout for an event total of 1 inch of snow. Otherwise a CoCoRaHS observer in Maynardville recorded 1.6 inches of snow, and a trained spotter recorded 1 inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow was recorded across Anderson County, with CoCoRaHS observers reporting 1.6 inches of snow in Norris, 1.1 inches and 1.2 inches of snow in Oak Ridge. Additionally the public reported 1.2 inches of snow in Oak Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by to Tennessee's south, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Knox County. CoCoRaHS reports of snowfall were: 2.6 inches in Knoxville, 1.5 inches in Knoxville, 1 inch in Seymour, 2 inches in Knoxville, 1.7 inches in Karns, and 2 inches in Farragut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of June 6, 2025. Damaging winds were the primary hazard with these thunderstorms with several reports of downed trees received. One thunderstorm near Spring Hill did produce an EF-0 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved through portions of Spring Hill during the evening of June 6th. The tornado started just west of Ray Williams Drive, producing damage throughout a subdivision just north of Saturn Parkway along Reserve Boulevard. The tornado then crossed Saturn Parkway, moving east along Jim Warren Road. It crossed I-65 and ended along Jim Warren Road just prior to Crafton Road. Damage along the path consisted primarily of trees uprooted or snapped as well as missing large branches. A few instances of structural damage were noted, mainly some shingles off of roofs, small pieces of metal fascia torn off, a few broken windows, and damage to siding due to blown debris. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds caused a tree to fall onto the road at the intersection of Baker Road and Melton Road in Monteagle. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during the April 2- April 5 event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harris Fork Creek flooded parts of Harris. Several homes were affected.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Obion River flooded parts of Obion. About a dozen homes were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally high atmospheric moisture values continued to lead to scattered thunderstorms across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of July 14, 2025. With very weak steering flow, the combination of heavy rain and slow storm motions led to flash flooding across portions of Davidson and Wilson counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Hermitage area, including on Old Hickory Boulevard in front of The Hermitage as well as on Tulip Grove Road near Chandler Road and Stoners Creek. Extent of damage is unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Green Hill area. Extent of damages is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms once again impacted Middle Tennessee on July 16, 2025. Lightning from one of these thunderstorms struck a natural gas substation in Davidson County which caused a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a natural gas substation in Joelton, causing a fire. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe supercell thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours on May 2nd, 2005 ahead of a pre-frontal trough axis moving towards central Tennessee. A few of these storms produced large hail and damaging winds, especially near the Lynchburg and Decherd areas. By the mid to late afternoon hours, storms weakened as a severe line of thunderstorms continued to develop and push east across much of Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in the city of Decherd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree is down across Hwy 412 near Hwy 152.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough oriented over the Midwest, ejected a cold front towards the Mid-South. Ahead of this boundary, the Mid-South was positioned within the left entrance region of a strong midlevel jet. This feature provided increased ascent through the afternoon. Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. As this activity moved southeast, storms congealed into broken line segments, creating a greater damaging wind threat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located just north of the Mid-South during the afternoon of July 17, 2025. A very hot air mass was present south of the boundary with enough moisture to support isolated thunderstorms. A slow-moving storm tracked from northeast to southwest across Shelby County, resulting in flash flooding and some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Moriah Road and Clarke Rd were shut down due to flash flooding and water overtopping roads, leaving them impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down onto a house at 2394 Penile Hill Road.

Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. As this activity moved southeast, storms congealed into broken line segments, creating a greater damaging wind threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs downed across the Purdy area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime airmass across Middle Tennessee led to the development of scattered afternoon thunderstorms on July 21, 2025. One of these thunderstorms in Nashville became briefly severe with a couple of trees downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a tree along with numerous branches onto Randall Drive near Oak Hill. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime airmass across Middle Tennessee led to the development of scattered afternoon thunderstorms on July 21, 2025. One of these thunderstorms in Nashville became briefly severe with a couple of trees downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds downed a tree along New York Avenue in west Nashville. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and instability led to the development of scattered thunderstorms across Middle Tennessee on July 22, 2025. One particular thunderstorm near Lawrenceburg downed several trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in the Lawrenceburg area, including one that fell on a car along 4th Street. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down onto power lines along Little Hurricane Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of July 17, 2025. A thunderstorm in Stewart County downed a few trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moving through the Dover area downed a few trees at a campground in the area. Size of the trees downed is unknown. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station north of McEwen recorded a maximum heat index of 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree is down on powerlines at Yellow Springs Rd and Hohammer Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough oriented over the Midwest, ejected a cold front towards the Mid-South. Ahead of this boundary, the Mid-South was positioned within the left entrance region of a strong midlevel jet. This feature provided increased ascent through the afternoon. Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon of July 9, 2025. Weak steering flow meant that these storms were generally slow-moving. This combined with above average atmospheric moisture meant that the stage was set for heavy rain and flooding. One thunderstorm in Lawrence and Wayne counties produced four to six inches of heavy rain which caused flooding in the Loretto, St. Joseph, and Iron City areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm produced 4 to 6 inches of rain in southern Lawrence County, causing flash flooding in the Loretto, St. Joseph, and Iron City. Reports of flooding included across Fairview Road in Loretto, along South 2nd Avenue near South Lawrence Elementary School, along TN-277, and flooding inside the Iron City Fire Department. Damages are a rough estimate.

By the mid to late afternoon hours, storms weakened as a severe line of thunderstorms continued to develop and push east across much of Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in the city of Winchester.

This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees snapped causing minor damage to the roof and trim of a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved out of central Arkansas and into the Mid-South during the mid-morning hours on June 7, 2025. The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down in Bolivar and across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dewpoints of 70 degrees played a significant role in reducing convective inhibition across the Mid-South, as well as enhancing low-level CAPE for storm initiation. 0-6km shear was not particularly strong, generally less than 25 knots, but increased to around 35 kts with a passing shortwave. Storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees down in  the Oakland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms continued throughout the day as an upper trough lifted slowly northeast across the region. A very moist airmass with PWATs near the 99th percentile, supported heavy rainfall resulting in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webb Town Rd near Arlan Reasons Rd near Cypress Creek were impassable. Additional roads flooded included Hwy 189 and Hwy 412.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional areas of tree damage were noted across the state line into Hardin County, Tennessee. Subsequent damage surveys and supplementary satellite imagery will help determine an accurate endpoint of the tornado after it extended into Tennessee. Estimated peak winds were around 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down. Time estimated by radar.

These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.

A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index value of 105 degrees.

This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak MCV moved slowly from the Southern Plains into the Mid-South and resulted in increased storm coverage during the afternoon and evening hours. The unstable and moist airmass supported some stronger storms with gusty winds and locally heavy rainfall and isolated flooding. The airmass was very moist with PWs approaching 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was some yard flooding from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of showers and thunderstorms occurred late Monday night into Tuesday morning just ahead of a cold front.  Combined lift and better shear resulted in a few strong storms. Moisture pooled, resulting in PWATs climbing above 2 inches areawide, which led to high rainfall rates and some minor flooding in urbanized areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Pam Drive was struck by lightning and quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds under a building upper-level ridge pushed air temperatures into the mid 90s along with oppressive humidity. Heat indices peaked in the range of 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon hours. Afternoon convective coverage over performed, and portions of West Tennessee were spared from the oppressive heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just east of the Bluegill Grill and Marina, producing sporadic tree damage between Holiday Drive and Awalt Road. At this location, the tornado was approximately 100 yards wide, snapping and uprooting several hardwood trees. From here, the tornado continued to the east-southeast, producing sporadic tree damage on Rock Creek Road and again just after crossing Highway 41A. Several hardwood and softwood trees were snapped and uprooted on Taylor Road. Sporadic tree damage continued north and east of Estill Springs, with several areas of large branches downed along the path. The tornado dissipated east of Hwy 41A.

This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was knocked down at 307 Guant Street in Petersburg.

In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during the April 2- April 5 event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire community of Rives was flooded due to three levee breeches caused by heavy rainfall. Nearly 100 structures were affected.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Fork Forked Deer River flooded across the southern part of Dyersburg. Water rescues from homes in South Dyersburg were necessary. Over 100 structures were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail in Pinson.

A longwave trough oriented over the Midwest, ejected a cold front towards the Mid-South. Ahead of this boundary, the Mid-South was positioned within the left entrance region of a strong midlevel jet. This feature provided increased ascent through the afternoon. Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. As this activity moved southeast, storms congealed into broken line segments, creating a greater damaging wind threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail near Airways/Hwy 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place across the Mid-South ahead of an approaching cold front. Dewpoints ranged from 75 to 80 degrees across the area. As the cold front sagged south, an upper-level disturbance pushed into the region, causing an increase in shear. Convection developed along an old outflow boundary during the late afternoon and evening hours on June 18, 2025. Additional development occurred as the front and additional outflow boundaries pushed south into the area. Storm mode was generally clusters and segments at first. Robust instability and high DCAPE values supported large hail and damaging winds initially. After midnight and during the early morning hours of June 19, 2025, the front nearly stalled and became parallel to the upper-level flow. As a result, convection became more east-west oriented with very heavy rainfall rates. Training storms caused significant flash flooding over parts of West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a house in the 500 block of Shotwell Street near Southern Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds under a building upper-level ridge pushed air temperatures into the mid 90s along with oppressive humidity. Heat indices peaked in the range of 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon hours. Afternoon convective coverage over performed, and portions of West Tennessee were spared from the oppressive heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This large tornado caused widespread damage across Grand Junction, including rail cars being blown off the tracks, downed trees, and roofs of homes. Along Highway 57, large sections of roofs were blown off. This tornado then continued northeast through a heavily wooded area in Hardeman County before causing more damage along West Fork Road, north of Saulsbury. High-resolution satellite imagery tracked a sporadic tree damage path across remote areas between West Road and Sain Road, with a more defined track picking up at Lake Hardeman Road and Maxwell Road. Additional damage was found to the east-northeast, with the tornado crossing Highway 125 and causing significant damage to a small community on Lake Vonda. The tornado lifted just before reaching Hebron. Peak winds were estimated between 120-130 mph in Hardeman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index value of 105 degrees.
