EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado crossed into Obion County causing damage to trees and homes at Mason Hall Kenton Roads. Some of the most intense damage occurred along Concord Road, with multiple trees snapped near their tops and uprooted, significant roof damage to multiple barn outbuildings, and damage to the roof and siding of a single-family home. As the tornado crossed Highway 45W, multiple homes had minor roof damage with several trees snapped near their tops or uprooted. The tornado continued eastward through several fields, with additional damage near Macedonia Road and Old Sidonia Kenton Road. The roof of a home was damaged with trees uprooted in this area. Additionally, a farm outbuilding was destroyed along Old Sidonia Kenton Road with the debris flown several hundred yards through nearby fields up to Sidonia Kenton Road. Multiple homes along these roads had minor roof damage, with multiple trees uprooted in the area. The tornado lifted shortly after crossing Sidonia Kenton Road. Peak winds were estimated to be 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index value of 105 degrees.

A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads impassable due to water throughout the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived tornado touched down just south of Denmark Jackson Road, leading to some minor roof damage to multiple homes along Denmark Jackson Road and Vine Hill Road. Some trees in the area were either uprooted or had large tree limbs broken as well. As the tornado moved up Vine Hill Road, a tree was uprooted, falling on a mobile home. Additional tree damage was noted in the area before the tornado lifted just south of Womack Lane. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado began in northeast Weakley County along Highway 89. Moving northeast, the tornado moved into far southeast Graves County, KY. In Tennessee, the tornado did extensive tree damage and damaged an outbuilding along Old Bethel Road. Peak winds were estimated to be 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough oriented over the Midwest, ejected a cold front towards the Mid-South. Ahead of this boundary, the Mid-South was positioned within the left entrance region of a strong midlevel jet. This feature provided increased ascent through the afternoon. Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. As this activity moved southeast, storms congealed into broken line segments, creating a greater damaging wind threat.

A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds under a building upper-level ridge pushed air temperatures into the mid 90s along with oppressive humidity. Heat indices peaked in the range of 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon hours. Afternoon convective coverage over performed, and portions of West Tennessee were spared from the oppressive heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hardeman County, the tornado moved through an inaccessible forested area south of Kennedy Road. Observed damage returned along Nubbin Ridge Road and Gentry Chapel Road, where numerous trees were uprooted and snapped. More trees were downed as the tornado crossed Highway 125 near Winwood Farms Loop and along Neely Road, where a home sustained minor roof damage and a small barn suffered a major loss of roof panels. The last damage was observed on Neely Road just south of Highway 57. Peak winds were estimated at 90-100 mph in Hardeman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail south of I-40 and the 45 bypass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just west of the Weakley County line, east of Ralston Road between the Ruthville and Chestnut Glade communities. Multiple trees were uprooted along a field, with a metal barn completely destroyed along Vaughn Road. A nearby home had minor roof damage as well. The tornado continued eastward causing additional tree damage and damage to homes on Chestnut Glade Road. One home had the majority of its roof damaged in this area, with multiple surrounding trees either uprooted or snapped about midway up the tree. The tornado continued toward Mac Terrell Road, where the most significant damage occurred. The roof of a single family home was severely damaged, with a nearby manufactured home completely shifted from its foundation and close to the road. A nearby garage had multiple walls and the roof caved inward on the structure. Most of the trees in the area were either uprooted or snapped near their tops. The tornado continued through multiple fields, with additional tree damage noted. A home along Highway 118 sustained greater than 20 percent damage to its roof. As the tornado continued northeast, additional tree tops were snapped or trees uprooted with some minor roof damage to a home and barn outbuilding along Bill Nanney Road. The tornado lifted shortly thereafter, before the Austin Springs community. Peak winds were estimated at 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system strengthened as it moved across southern Missouri into western Kentucky thanks to surface heating and increasing instability. This system produced an outflow boundary, causing damaging winds across parts of West Tennessee. The mesoscale convective system eventually sagged into Henry County, TN, resulting in large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds under a building upper-level ridge pushed air temperatures into the mid 90s along with oppressive humidity. Heat indices peaked in the range of 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon hours. Afternoon convective coverage over performed, and portions of West Tennessee were spared from the oppressive heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down near the intersection of S Fishing road and Boonshill Petersburg road. Time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered supercells developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the late afternoon and evening hours. Large hail was reported in portions of southern middle Tennessee with a few storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough oriented over the Midwest, ejected a cold front towards the Mid-South. Ahead of this boundary, the Mid-South was positioned within the left entrance region of a strong midlevel jet. This feature provided increased ascent through the afternoon. Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. As this activity moved southeast, storms congealed into broken line segments, creating a greater damaging wind threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail along the Crockett/Lauderdale county line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed near Locke Road and quickly intensified as it moved to the east-northeast just north of Sulphur Springs. It produced mainly tree damage along Eldie Foster and Nelson Store Roads. The tornado produced significant damage to several homes on Sulphur Springs Road near Dee Moore Road and destroyed a manufactured home. The tornado then moved over a more forested area south of Ervin Hester Road, but a well-defined swath of downed trees can be seen via aerial imagery. The tornado intensified as it moved across Highway 45 into Selmer, damaging numerous homes on the western and northern portions of town. The most significant damage was surveyed along New Bethel Road and Adams Extension, where several homes were destroyed. Wind speed estimates in this area were 145-160 mph, corresponding to a high-end EF3 rating. Between these locations, damage to homes and trees was generally in the EF2 range. Significant damage continued to be observed east of the Illinois Central Railroad tracks along Bethesda Purdy Road, where a home suffered major roof damage and a metal building was destroyed. The tornado tracked further northeast, producing minor to significant tree damage, but affected a few more homes and outbuildings along Purdy Road and Airport Road. Aerial imagery revealed significant tree damage between Purdy Road and Barnes Road, with damage to trees and outbuildings noted near the intersection of Old Stage Road and Leapwood Enville Road. Consistent tree damage continued as the tornado crossed Puron Road, Neely Sharp Road, and Highway 22 north of Adamsville before crossing into Hardin County. Peak winds were estimated at 160 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved out of central Arkansas and into the Mid-South during the mid-morning hours on June 7, 2025. The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down on Highway 70 near Aztec Drive in Gallaway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dewpoints of 70 degrees played a significant role in reducing convective inhibition across the Mid-South, as well as enhancing low-level CAPE for storm initiation. 0-6km shear was not particularly strong, generally less than 25 knots, but increased to around 35 kts with a passing shortwave. Storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on power lines along Hwy 70 near Cedar Grove. Nickel sized hail also falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms continued throughout the day as an upper trough lifted slowly northeast across the region. A very moist airmass with PWATs near the 99th percentile, supported heavy rainfall resulting in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported several roads closed across Carroll County. Brewer Rd was washed out.

Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several downed trees across Old Paris Murray Rd, north of Hwy 140 E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down at 2010 Thornton Taylor Parkway. Time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree trunks were snapped, and an outpost building was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covering up to 2 lanes on I-40 east bound, before the Watkins exits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system strengthened as it moved across southern Missouri into western Kentucky thanks to surface heating and increasing instability. This system produced an outflow boundary, causing damaging winds across parts of West Tennessee. The mesoscale convective system eventually sagged into Henry County, TN, resulting in large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines down on Highway 114 along the Carroll and Henry County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system strengthened as it moved across southern Missouri into western Kentucky thanks to surface heating and increasing instability. This system produced an outflow boundary, causing damaging winds across parts of West Tennessee. The mesoscale convective system eventually sagged into Henry County, TN, resulting in large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down across Holiday Hills Lane in Counce, TN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across the road at 280 Crystal Ridge road east of Mulberry. Time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms formed along and ahead of a pre-front trough axis that move along a stalled front well to our north on June 6th. One storm in Lincoln county produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees down in Petersburg. Time  estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another hot and humid day for the Mid-South. A stationary cold front was situated just north of the AR/MO and TN/KY state lines, which trapped the region on the hot and humid side of the boundary. Afternoon heat indices ranged from 105 to 109 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Oppressive heat continued to plague the Mid-South as an intense ridge retrograded across the Lower Mississippi Valley. Highs ranged from the upper 90s to the lower 100s. Dewpoints across the region soared into the upper 70s and low 80s. This combination led to dangerous heat indices greater than 110 degrees nearly areawide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 111 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree  was reported down at 171 Old Lincoln road blocking one lane of traffic. Time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge began to build over the Mid-South, causing slightly above normal temperatures for early July. Heat indices peaked between 95 and 100 degrees F. Hot and humid nights made heat stress an increasing threat. A high school athlete collapsed at football practice on July 6th and was hospitalized and began treatment for heat stroke. Unfortunately, he succumbed to complications from organ failure caused by severe heat stroke and passed away on July 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high school athlete collapsed at football practice. He was hospitalized and treated for heat stroke until he succumbed to complications from organ failure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across the road at 144 Golden Hollow Road. Time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east into southern middle Tennessee (north of a slow moving frontal boundary edge north from central Alabama) interacted with an unstable atmosphere in the mid-afternoon hours to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed east into eastern Tennessee between 6 and 7 PM. This line of storms produced damaging winds across a good portion of southern middle Tennessee, particularly in northern portions of Lincoln, Moore and NW Franklin counties. In addition, a brief QLCS tornado occurred as well in Franklin county, TN.||This area was worked over by this initial convection enough to keep additional severe weather from occurring with a second line of thunderstorms that developed further south later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down covering the whole roadway. Time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms affected the far northeast Tennessee valley and southwest Virginia on the evening of the 30th, producing some flash flooding across those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Scannerfood Facebook page relayed a report of shed collapsing due to floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms affected the far northeast Tennessee valley and southwest Virginia on the evening of the 30th, producing some flash flooding across those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Scannerfood Facebook page shared reports of a small sinkhole opening up on Kitzmiller Road at the railroad pass due to flooding. It slowly expanded in size between 8:00 PM and 9:30 PM EDT before local government personnel closed that portion of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat and humidity persisted across the Mid-South. Heat indices ranged between 105 and 109 across West Tennessee as an upper-level ridge continued to build, nudging temperatures and heat indices upwards daily.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough slowly crossing the region was able to pull sufficient Gulf moisture into West Tennessee. This moisture combined with 90 degree air temperatures was enough for heat index values to exceed 105 degrees. Afternoon shower and thunderstorm coverage was minimal and only provided temporary relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, stationary heat dome brought oppressive heat to the Mid-South in mid to late August. The region was fed by warm, moist air from the Gulf, which elevated dewpoints and thus made heat-index values much higher than the actual air temperature alone. Because cloud cover was suppressed and the ground surface was dry, nighttime cooling was limited, elevating heat stress. The heat dome remained parked for several days, resulting in minimal forecast change or relief. Finally, an upper-level disturbance was able to bring relief as showers and thunderstorm chances returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 107 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed during the early to mid afternoon hours. One of the thunderstorms spawned a rare August tornado, knocking down numerous trees along a short path along Little Crow Creek in Southern Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down on Sinking Cove Road causing several uprooted trees. It briefly tracked south-southeast uprooting additional trees, passing across Little Crow Creek before lifting|just northwest of The Bolo Club on Sinking Cove Road where no additional tree damage was noted. Damage was assessed via drone footage taken by Jackson County Alabama Emergency Management in|coordination with Franklin County Tennessee Emergency Management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Upper Finley Road on the northwest side of Dyersburg and tracked east-northeast across the northern part of the city. The damage consisted mainly of fallen trees and power lines, minor roof damage, and minor structural damage. Sixteen houses were damaged along with one public building and seven businesses. The tornado lifted near David Road near the Highway 51 and Highway 412 junction. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South including the Memphis Metropolitan area where over 20 car rescues were performed. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and a small outbuilding were damaged during a squall line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, stationary heat dome brought oppressive heat to the Mid-South in mid to late August. The region was fed by warm, moist air from the Gulf, which elevated dewpoints and thus made heat-index values much higher than the actual air temperature alone. Because cloud cover was suppressed and the ground surface was dry, nighttime cooling was limited, elevating heat stress. The heat dome remained parked for several days, resulting in minimal forecast change or relief. Finally, an upper-level disturbance was able to bring relief as showers and thunderstorm chances returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a weak mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) propagated south over the Mississippi River in the early afternoon hours. The lift from the MCV combined with instability and increasing tropical moisture, resulted in a few showers and thunderstorms. Coverage was isolated. The increasing moisture fueled torrential downpours over urbanized areas, leading to a quick flash flooding threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations in Midtown Memphis had water over the road, impeding traffic.  Lemaster Street and the Crosstown area were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin dominated the upper-level pattern and steered the remnants of an MCS directly towards the Mid-South. Shower and thunderstorm coverage was much higher than in previous days as storms tapped into moderate to high instability. Storms were able to quickly grow upscale and produce damaging winds and some sub-severe hail. Several outflow boundaries were produced from convective coverage with measured 40-50 mph winds associated with the boundaries. These winds were enough to produce sporadic damage in addition to stronger winds from severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed due to an outflow boundary. A car then crashed into the downed tree as it was on a roadway. Crash occurred on E Pkwy, just north of Christian Brothers University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dipped into the -1 to 5 above range at a few locations during the mornings of the 14th and 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped into the zero to 5 degrees above zero range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions created a cumulus field and supported convective initiation. A pulse storm developed in southeastern Shelby County and produced localized tree damage and small hail.
Hot and humid conditions created a cumulus field and supported convective initiation. A pulse storm developed in southeastern Shelby County and produced localized tree damage and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree downed blocking the intersection of Holmes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during the April 2- April 5 event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event including major flooding on the Obion River and the North Fork Forked Deer River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along the Rutherford Fork Obion River. Three homes were affected on the Dyer County side of Kenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low helped advect near record moisture into Tennessee, where a boundary and classic heavy rain profiles combined to produce extreme rainfall rates over portions of Chattanooga, leading to numerous water rescues, a few evacuations, flooded interstate and local roads, flooded homes, and unfortunately a few fatalities. One fatality occurred in the east Brainerd area after a driver went around police barricades and was swept away in the flood waters. Damage estimates as of September 2025 were at least $1.7 million dollars per emergency management.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waterlogged soils caused a large oak tree to fall and crush a car, killing the family of 3 within the vehicle. Calm to near calm winds were observed at the time of the incident off the nearby Chattanooga ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos from social media posts showed street flooding along Ringgold Road in multiple locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was estimated to be 3 feet deep near Shallowford and Gunbarrel roads, with with water going over the hood of a truck. Additionally, someone drove around police barricades and into flood waters on nearby East Brainard Road before being swept away. Unfortunately this resulted in a fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low helped advect near record moisture into Tennessee, where a boundary and classic heavy rain profiles combined to produce extreme rainfall rates over portions of Chattanooga, leading to numerous water rescues, a few evacuations, flooded interstate and local roads, flooded homes, and unfortunately a few fatalities. One fatality occurred in the east Brainerd area after a driver went around police barricades and was swept away in the flood waters. Damage estimates as of September 2025 were at least $1.7 million dollars per emergency management.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TDOT Smartway camera photos showed significant flooding on Interstate 24 at McBrien Road. Vehicles that entered the floodwater in the recent half hour have been abandoned, with drivers crossing the interstate divider and being helped to higher. Later photos and videos from social media showed cars floating on the interstate in this same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, stationary heat dome brought oppressive heat to the Mid-South in mid to late August. The region was fed by warm, moist air from the Gulf, which elevated dewpoints and thus made heat-index values much higher than the actual air temperature alone. Because cloud cover was suppressed and the ground surface was dry, nighttime cooling was limited, elevating heat stress. The heat dome remained parked for several days, resulting in minimal forecast change or relief. Finally, an upper-level disturbance was able to bring relief as showers and thunderstorm chances returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 108 degrees.

This moisture combined with 90 degree air temperatures was enough for heat index values to exceed 105 degrees. Afternoon shower and thunderstorm coverage was minimal and only provided temporary relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2025 saw marked improvement in drought conditions across West Tennessee, driven mainly by above-average rainfall, especially during the final week of October. While severe drought (D2) and moderate drought (D1) continued in parts of the region at times during the month, the overall trend was toward diminished drought intensity by late October. A few counties near the Tennessee River persisted D2 conditions through the entire month, but the swath of the D2 continuously shrunk in size each week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions persisted across portions of Obion county through mid October before improving to Moderate Drought (D1). Abnormally Dry (D0) Conditions were found at the end of October.

A deepening upper trough and a surface low near the Mississippi River, combined with a cold air dam on the North Carolina side of the mountains, allowed for strong non-thunderstorm winds to spill over in the Smokies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cove Mountain weather station in the Great Smoky Mountains National Park recorded two wind gusts, a 58 mph gust at the 10 AM EDT observation, and a 59.5 mph wind gust at the 12 PM EDT observation. These were non-thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving front pushed into the Mid-South during the evening hours of October 18th. Copious amounts of shear combined with a moderate amount of instability resulted in a line of thunderstorms along and ahead of the front that produced isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was knocked down on Barham Street south of Ash Street in Jackson. The power line knocked a large branch off a tree as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an abnormally dry September, beneficial rainfall returned to Middle Tennessee during October 2025. Rain amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches during the month which resulted in improvements in the drought status. The 10 percent of the area that was in severe drought status at the end of September improved to 0 percent by early October. By the end of the month, there was only moderate drought in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions during September 2025 improved to moderate drought in early October across Montgomery County. By the end of the month, no drought conditions were present across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers and strong to severe thunderstorms (a few exhibiting embedded supercell structures) pushed east into northwestern Alabama during the  early evening hours.  This line became more organized and stronger as it pushed quickly east as it approached the I-65 corridor. producing more widespread 50 to 70 mph wind gusts.  The winds resulted in trees and power lines being knocked down at numerous locations and several thousand without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down at Vanzant road and  MCBURG Dellrose road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry December 2025 resulted in worsening drought conditions across Middle Tennessee. Rain totals across the area generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches which was below normal for the month. But the biggest rain deficit was measured across the far eastern Cumberland Plateau. Crossville measured only 1.44 inches of rain during the month which was 3.90 inches below normal. By the end of December, 44 percent of Middle Tennessee was classified as moderate drought with about 2 percent of that in severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across eastern Cumberland County at the end of December 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold front brought a line of showers and thunderstorms that were able to successfully transport strong to severe wind gusts to the surface in portions of East Tennessee. Additionally, winds aloft ahead of the front produced several instances of high wind criteria non-thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was broken in two by severe thunderstorm wind gusts, as well as several large tree branches and a few shingles off a nearby roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold front brought a line of showers and thunderstorms that were able to successfully transport strong to severe wind gusts to the surface in portions of East Tennessee. Additionally, winds aloft ahead of the front produced several instances of high wind criteria non-thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain Fire Tower wind instrumentation recorded wind advisory gusts from non-thunderstorm winds between 4 PM LST and 2 AM LST, with high wind criteria gusts between 6 PM and 1 AM. The peak gust occurred at 11 PM LST with a magnitude of 78 mph. The observation tower also recorded a 77 mph gust on its midnight reporting time, which coincides with passage of the convective line. Due to the strength of the ongoing winds at the Mountain, it is unclear if the 12 AM observation was aided by the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Middle Tennessee during the evening of December 28, 2025, brought a line of showers and thunderstorms into the area. Gusty winds were associated with this activity, and while they generally remained sub-severe, a couple trees were reported down in the Springfield area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed trees on Highway 161 near Boyd Holland Road in Springfield. Over 1,000 customers were without power. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November precipitation was below average with rainfall being sparse in the first three weeks of the month, with most falling later on. The limited November rain slowed or partially offset improvements achieved by rainfall in October, however, because October had been wetter than average, drought did not fully redevelop in much of the immediate Memphis metro by month���s end. All of West Tennessee improved to Abnormally Dry conditions or no drought conditions by December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small swath of Severe (D2) Drought conditions improved by 2 classes by month's end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a cold front as it pushed through the Tennessee River into Middle Tennessee. Enough instability and shear were present to support a few severe storms from near the Tennessee River eastward into Middle Tennessee. Some wind damage occurred in Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief microburst caused damage to tree limbs, uprooted a tree, and damaged a barn along Cedar Grove Road and Holland Road. Small hail also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave moved across the middle Mississippi Valley and into the lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of November 18, 2025. Meanwhile, a surface low tracked from northern Missouri into western Kentucky. The associated cold front pushed into northwest Tennessee during the evening hours. A few thunderstorms developed and became severe as the front intersected an instability axis over northwest Tennessee. These severe storms produced large hail over northwest Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening of November 7, 2025. With warm, moist air ahead of a cold front along with abundant wind shear, this environment allowed for damaging wind gusts along the leading edge of the thunderstorms. Several reports of downed trees were received along with a few reports of hail up the size of quarters. With waning instability, these thunderstorms weakened as they got east of Nashville and closer to the Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a tree onto a house on W 1st Street in Dickson. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening of November 7, 2025. With warm, moist air ahead of a cold front along with abundant wind shear, this environment allowed for damaging wind gusts along the leading edge of the thunderstorms. Several reports of downed trees were received along with a few reports of hail up the size of quarters. With waning instability, these thunderstorms weakened as they got east of Nashville and closer to the Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds were reported in the Bellevue area by multiple broadcast meteorologists and storm chasers. A few trees were reported down in the area. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in September, soil moisture was well below normal, and river flows were below typical values due to the lack of beneficial rainfall. Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded and worsened to Extreme (D3) Drought for a brief period during September.  Pastures were providing very little or no feed, and supplemental feeding was required to maintain livestock. Welcomed rainfall was not enough to remove drought conditions entirely, but D3 conditions improved and D2 conditions continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions were introduced in early September. Drought conditions encompassed most of the county and continued into early October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave generated light precipitation that resulted in a light snowfall across higher elevations of the Tennessee Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of four inches of snow fell at 3,800 feet in Roan Mountain, TN over the course of the day. Only 0.8 inches of snow was recorded at 2,800 feet in Roan Mountain State Park.

Additionally, winds aloft ahead of the front produced several instances of high wind criteria non-thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorm wind gusts blew off a roof of a metal pole barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening of September 3, 2025. While these thunderstorms remained non-severe due to poor lapse rates, other ingredients were enough to still allow for strong winds. These thunderstorms also produced frequent lightning which was the cause for a structure fire in Mt. Juliet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds downed a tree in Bellevue near Bellevue Road off Highway 70. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a structure fire in the 500 building of Glass Creek Apartments. The fire was contained to a small portion of the roof. No injuries were reported, and no residents were displaced. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front swept through Middle Tennessee during the evening of September 5, 2025. With an unstable and moist atmosphere ahead of this feature, the front provided additional lift to aid in the development of thunderstorms. These thunderstorms were able to become severe, producing large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported during this event was tennis ball size hail in Gainesboro with numerous reports of downed trees received throughout Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a tree on Cypress Creek Road near Drop Anchor RV Park in Linden. The entire road was blocked. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry September 2025 led to worsening drought conditions, especially across portions of northwest Middle Tennessee. Precipitation totals during the month generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches across northwest Middle Tennessee which saw the least amount of rain through September. The Clarksville ASOS measured only 1.09 inches of rain during the month which was 2.38 inches below normal. Drought conditions did briefly deteriorate to extreme drought across Stewart and Montgomery counties, however, some beneficial rain during the latter half of September improved conditions back to severe drought. By the end of September, about 26 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least moderate drought with 10 percent of this in severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions briefly developed across Maury County during the first week of September 2025.

Drought conditions did briefly deteriorate to extreme drought across Stewart and Montgomery counties, however, some beneficial rain during the latter half of September improved conditions back to severe drought. By the end of September, about 26 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least moderate drought with 10 percent of this in severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across Montgomery County during September 2025. Drought conditions did deteriorate to extreme drought briefly during the middle of the month, but improved back to severe before September ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough along with a favorable pressure gradient and nighttime inversion allowed strong winds aloft to reach the East Tennessee Smoky Mountains, producing high winds in a small portion of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cove Mountain Fire Tower in GSMNP recorded a 59 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust at the 4 AM observation, and a 64 mph gust at the 5 AM observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave generated light precipitation that resulted in a light snowfall across higher elevations of the Tennessee Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow fell at 5,000 feet elevation at Newfound Gap and 4 inches of snow fell at 6,400 feet of elevation on Mount LeConte. Both locations reside in the Great Smoky Mountains National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold front brought a line of showers and thunderstorms that were able to successfully transport strong to severe wind gusts to the surface in portions of East Tennessee. Additionally, winds aloft ahead of the front produced several instances of high wind criteria non-thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several powerlines were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was situated across the Mid-South during the evening of September 23, 2025. Deep moisture combined with sufficient shear and instability near the front resulted in large clusters of showers and thunderstorms.  These slow-moving storms produced very heavy rain and caused flash flooding in the Memphis metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported at Old Brownsville Road and Billy Maher Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developing during the morning of the 27th across eastern Tennessee, lasting into the early afternoon hours. The slow moving nature of these storms produced some rainfall totals in the 3 to 5 inch range for portions of Hawkins and Sevier counties, leading to some reports of flooding there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed a small stream out of its banks and flooding over driveways on  Amis Chapel Road. Time of video unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm ahead of a cold front was able to become surface based and produce severe winds over a significant swath of Carter County, despite the early morning timeframe of the storm's passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of intermittent thunderstorm wind damage due to a severe convective downdraft. Wind damage began in portions of Elizabethton, mainly confined to trees and powerlines being blown down, and continued parallel to SR-91 to the county line with Johnson County. A trampoline from an unknown address was lofted and eventually deposited outside of a small grocer on the east side of Elizabethton. SR-91 was blocked in multiple locations by fallen trees, including near the Stoney Creek VFD Station Number 1.
